claims.claim: T2 is identified as the trigger temperature of thermal runaway, defined as the demarcation point where the temperature rise rate reaches 1 °C/s, beyond which a series of uncontrollable reactions commence.
claims.quote: T2 is identified as the trigger temperature of the TR, and it is also the demarcation point of the sharp rise in temperature, where the temperature rise rate reaches 1 °C s−1. Beyond T2, a series of uncontrollable reactions commence.

claims.claim: Type 2 ISC (anode connected to aluminum current collector) is the most dangerous ISC configuration and always leads to thermal runaway, while Type 3 (cathode-Cu) and Type 4 (cathode-anode) do not lead to thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Type 2 is the most dangerous ISC type in which the anode connects with Al current collector, followed by type 1 ISC type in which the anode connects with the Cu current collector. These two ISC types always lead to TR. While in the type 3 ISC, the cathode connects with Cu current collector, and in type 4 ISC type, the cathode connects with the anode, and are favorite ISC types, which do not lead to TR.

claims.claim: Statistical data indicates that 52% of battery fire accidents are attributed to ISC events.
claims.quote: Statistical data indicates that 52% of battery fire accidents are attributed to ISC events.

claims.claim: When battery temperature reaches approximately 100 °C, SEI decomposes and the exposed lithium-embedded anode reacts with the electrolyte; at approximately 170 °C, the separator melts causing ISC; at approximately 200 °C, the cathode material begins to release oxygen.
claims.quote: When the temperature of the battery rises to about 100 °C under inappropriate application conditions, SEI decomposes and produces a large amount of flammable reducing gas. The exposed lithium-embedded anode reacts with the electrolyte, continuously releasing heat and releasing by-products. With the continuous increase of the internal temperature of the battery, it reaches the melting point of the separator at about 170 °C, which is easy to cause ISC and release a large amount of Joule heat. When the temperature reaches about 200 °C, the cathode material begins to release oxygen.

claims.claim: The thermal decomposition stability order of common cathode materials from least to most stable is: LCO < NCM < LMO < LFP.
claims.quote: Their thermal decomposition stability order, from least to most stable, is as follows: LCO < NCM < LMO < LFP.

claims.claim: At approximately 276 °C, a severe oxidation reaction occurs between O2 released from the cathode and the reduced LixC anode, accompanied by an almost seven-fold increase in heat generation.
claims.quote: apart from the serious phase transition and O2 release of the cathode material at ≈276 °C, there is a severe oxidation reaction between the released O2, which migrate from the cathode through the separator to the reduced LixC anode...The redox reaction is accompanied with an almost seven-fold increase in heat, generation.

claims.claim: During overdischarge, the increasing anode potential triggers oxidation of the copper current collector, producing Cu+ and Cu2+, which migrate to the cathode, are reduced to copper, and grow as copper dendrites that eventually penetrate the separator and induce ISC.
claims.quote: the increasing anode potential will trigger the oxidation of the Cu collector, producing Cu+ and Cu2+. The produced Cu+ and Cu2+ will reach to the cathode and be reduced to Cu. The continual reduction of Cu+ and Cu2+ promotes the growth of copper dendrites on the cathode, which eventually penetrating the separator and inducing ISC.

claims.claim: The initial decomposition temperature of LiPF6 in a humid atmosphere is reduced to about 87 °C, significantly lower than in a dry atmosphere, and its decomposition products in humid conditions include POF3 and HF gas in addition to LiF and PF5.
claims.quote: Yang et al. pointed out that the initial decomposition temperature of LiPF6 in humid atmosphere was significantly lower than that in a dry atmosphere, which was reduced to about 87 °C. In addition, the thermal decomposition products of LiPF6 in a humid atmosphere include POF3 and HF gas in addition to LiF and PF5.

claims.claim: Reductive gases from the anode are generated at temperatures below 80 °C and affect the cathode through crosstalk, causing the cathode material to undergo a phase transition below its original phase transition temperature and releasing a large amount of O2 and heat.
claims.quote: reductive gases from the anode are generated at temperatures below 80 °C and affect the cathode through crosstalk. This causes the cathode material to undergo a phase transition below its original phase transition temperature, releasing a large amount of O2 and heat.

claims.claim: Due to difficulty of internal temperature diffusion, the temperature difference between inside and outside of the battery was as high as 180 °C at 100% SOC, demonstrating serious delay and limitations in external temperature monitoring.
claims.quote: they demonstrated through experimental data that there are serious delay and limitations in the monitoring results by external temperature. Due to the difficulty of internal temperature diffusion, the temperature difference between inside and outside was as high as 180 °C at 100% SOC.

claims.claim: The SEI on the anode begins to decompose at around 80°C.
claims.quote: the solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) on the anode begins to decompose at around 80 ∘C

claims.claim: The electrolyte starts breaking down at 100–120°C.
claims.quote: the electrolyte starts breaking down at 100–120 ∘C

claims.claim: Polyethylene (PE) and polypropylene (PP) separators collapse at about 135°C.
claims.quote: polyethylene (PE) and polypropylene (PP) separators collapse at about 135 ∘C

claims.claim: Electrolyte breakdown releases flammable gases including H2, CH4, C2H6, and C2H4.
claims.quote: Electrolyte breakdown releases flammable gases like H2, CH4, C2H6, and C2H4

claims.claim: If the separator collapses, internal short circuits may occur, releasing stored electrical energy, heat, and gas, thus exacerbating the temperature increase and promoting further chain reactions until thermal runaway ensues.
claims.quote: If the separator collapses, internal short cir- cuits may occur, releasing stored electrical energy, heat, and gas, thus exacerbating the temperature increase and promoting further chain reactions until thermal runaway ensues.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway in a single cell within a battery pack can propagate to adjacent cells, potentially causing catastrophic failures.
claims.quote: thermal runaway in a single cell can propagate to adjacent cells, potentially causing catastrophic failures

claims.claim: Cylindrical and prismatic cells might rupture or explode during thermal runaway, while pouch cells are more likely to swell or catch fire.
claims.quote: cylindrical and prismatic cells might rupture or explode, while pouch cells are more likely to swell or catch fire

claims.claim: A pressure-responsive CID in cylindrical cells activates when internal pressure escalates to approximately 145 psi, causing the top disk to deform outward and break the electrical connection.
claims.quote: When the internal pressure in the bat- tery escalates to approximately 145 psi, the top disk deforms outward, forcing the central point upward and breaking the connection between the metallic foil and the top disk.

claims.claim: A CID may not operate correctly if the weld strength between the metallic foil and the top disk is too strong or too weak.
claims.quote: The CID may not operate correctly if the weld strength between the metallic foil and the top disk is too strong or too weak.

claims.claim: In cylindrical cells, the CID is a one-time protective device that permanently disables the battery once activated.
claims.quote: In cylindrical cells, the CID is a one-time protective device that permanently disables the battery once activated.

claims.claim: A thermal fuse operates within a temperature range of 85–120°C, ideally around 95°C ± 5°C, and melts at a predetermined temperature to break the electrical circuit and halt thermal runaway.
claims.quote: A thermal fuse, typically constructed from materials such as Sn, Bi, In, or Pb, operates within a temperature range of 85–120 ∘C, ideally around 95 ∘C ± 5 ∘C. Integrated into batteries, this fuse melts at a predetermined temperature, preemptively breaking the electrical circuit to halt thermal runaway

claims.claim: Shape memory alloy CIDs provide a reversible solution: during temperature spikes, a disk spring bends backward to cut off the electrical circuit, and as temperature normalizes, the spring returns to its original configuration, restoring the electrical connection.
claims.quote: In the event of temperature spikes from overcharging or short circuits, a disk spring within the shape memory alloy bends backward, effectively cutting off the electrical circuit to prevent fur- ther temperature escalation. As the temperature normalizes, the disk spring returns to its original configuration, restoring the electrical connection

claims.claim: PTC thermistors are typically absent in prismatic and pouch batteries but are commonly found in commercial cylindrical cell designs.
claims.quote: While commonly found in commercial cylindrical cell designs, PTC thermistors are typically absent in prismatic and pouch batteries

claims.claim: The conductive polymer in PTC thermistors exhibits low resistance in the milliohm range (1–10 mΩ) at room temperature.
claims.quote: a conductive polymer, which exhibits low resistance in the milliohm range at room temperature (1–10 mΩ)

claims.claim: When exposed to temperatures above 100°C from external short circuits, the conductive polymer in the PTC thermistor transitions to a high-resistance state, reducing the battery's current load.
claims.quote: When exposed to temperatures above 100 ∘C from external short circuits, the conductive polymer in the PTC thermistor transitions to a high-resistance state, reducing the battery's current load

claims.claim: Delays in the PTC's response to internal hot spots within the battery, such as at the SEI layer and electrodes, may occur due to the placement distance of the PTC from those locations.
claims.quote: delays in the PTC's response to internal hot spots within the battery, such as at the SEI layer and electrodes, may occur due to their placement distance

claims.claim: Safety vents for prismatic cells open at critical pressures of 113–128 psi (0.85 MPa), while the trigger pressure for 18650 cylindrical cells is 406 psi (2.8 MPa).
claims.quote: internal pressures reach critical levels, such as 113–128 psi (0.85 MPa) for prismatic cells and 406 psi (2.8 MPa) for 18650 cylindrical cells

claims.claim: The trigger pressure of safety vents for 18650 cylindrical cells at 406 psi generally exceeds that of pressure-responsive CIDs at 145 psi.
claims.quote: The trigger pressure of safety vents, set at 406 psi for 18650 cylindrical cells, generally exceeds that of pressure-responsive CIDs at 145 psi

claims.claim: If a vent becomes blocked and internal pressure cannot be released promptly, the escalating pressure may rupture the battery case or lead to an explosion.
claims.quote: if a vent becomes blocked and internal pressure cannot be released promptly, the escalating pressure may rupture the battery case or lead to an explosion

claims.claim: Acyclic carbonate solvents like DEC, DMC, and EMC are flammable due to their low flash points of 15–33°C.
claims.quote: acyclic solvents like diethyl carbonate (DEC), dimethyl carbonate (DMC), and ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC) are flammable due to their low flash points—15–33 ∘C

claims.claim: Effective flame retardant concentrations in electrolytes typically remain below 20%, with optimal values reported for specific FRs such as 10 wt% BMP-PF6, CDP, and IPPP.
claims.quote: Effective FR concentrations typically remain below 20%, with optimal values reported for specific FRs such as 10 wt% BMP-PF6, cresyl diphenyl phosphate (CDP), and 4-isopropyl phenyl diphenyl phosphate (IPPP)

claims.claim: A PVDF-HFP shell separator releases TPP flame retardant at around 160°C during early combustion stages without compromising battery electrochemical reactions.
claims.quote: releasing TPP at around 160 ∘C during early combustion stages without compromising battery electrochemical reactions

claims.claim: The PE middle layer in a triple-layered PP/PE/PP separator melts at around 130°C to stop ion flow and prevent overheating, while the PP outer layers provide mechanical integrity up to about 165°C.
claims.quote: The PE middle layer functions as the primary shutdown mechanism by melting at around 130 ∘C, effectively stopping ion flow to prevent overheating. With a higher melting point of about 165 ∘C, the PP outer layers provide mechanical strength and structural integrity

claims.claim: The shutdown separator function can provide little protection under rapid localized overheating, overcharging, or internal short circuits.
claims.quote: the shutdown function can provide little protection under rapid localized overheating, overcharging, or internal short circuits

claims.claim: Cylindrical cells with shutdown separators went into thermal runaway under overcharge conditions, possibly because inhomogeneous temperature distribution caused incomplete separator shutdown.
claims.quote: Although the shutdown separator has been implemented in cylindrical cells, the batteries went into thermal runaway under overcharge conditions. The possible reason was the inhomogeneity of the temperature distribution, causing an incomplete separator shutdown

claims.claim: The separator shutdown function can provide only a limited degree of protection for a single battery or a battery package consisting of a small number of batteries.
claims.quote: the separator shutdown function can provide a limited degree of protection for a single battery or battery package consisting of a small number of batteries

claims.claim: When an internal short-circuit event like nail penetration occurs in a metal-polymer current collector, the thermoplastic polymer substrate melts and shrinks away from the nail, disrupting the electric conduction path and reducing the chances of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: When an internal short-circuit event like nail penetration occurs, the thermo- plastic polymer substrate will melt and shrink away from the nail due to the heat generated. The melting causes the active material to lose support and split apart, disrupting the electric conduction path created by the short circuit and reducing the chances of thermal runaway.

claims.claim: Metal layers on metal-polymer current collectors are typically 0.5–1.0 μm thick.
claims.quote: The metal layers are typically 0.5–1.0 μm

claims.claim: Short-circuiting the two current collectors (anode Cu and cathode Al) leads to the most severe consequences under mechanical impact.
claims.quote: short-circuiting the two current collectors leads to the most severe consequences

claims.claim: Many BMS configurations do not measure the temperature of each cell, relying instead on sensors positioned across the battery pack.
claims.quote: many BMS configurations do not measure the temperature of each cell, relying instead on sensors positioned across the battery pack

claims.claim: It is generally advised that the BTMS should not exceed 20% of the total battery pack weight.
claims.quote: It is generally advised that the BTMS should not exceed 20% of the total battery pack weight

claims.claim: PCM cooling systems can be limited by their heat storage capacity and the need for cooling cycles to reset the PCM to its solid state.
claims.quote: they can be limited by their heat storage capacity and the need for cooling cycles to reset the PCM to its solid state

claims.claim: Air-cooling systems have relatively weak heat dissipation capability and significant dependence on ambient temperature compared to other cooling methods.
claims.quote: its limitations include relatively weak heat dissipation capability and significant dependence on ambient temperature

claims.claim: PTC thermistors are not present in prismatic and pouch cells, CIDs are commonly present in cylindrical and prismatic cells but not in pouch cells, and safety vents are commonly present in cylindrical and prismatic cells but not in pouch cells.
claims.quote: PTC: Present in some commercial cells [coin], Commonly present [cylindrical], Not present [prismatic], Not present [pouch]. CID: Present in some commercial cells [coin], Commonly present [cylindrical], Commonly present [prismatic], Not present [pouch]. Safety vents: Not present [coin], Commonly present [cylindrical], Commonly present [prismatic], Not present [pouch].

claims.claim: LG Chem does not use a PTC in most of its cylindrical cells.
claims.quote: LG Chem does not use a PTC in most of its cylindrical cells

claims.claim: A PTC thermistor will slightly reduce cell capacity when included in a battery.
claims.quote: a PTC will slightly reduce the cell capacity

claims.claim: At 100% SOC without thermal management, the entire five-cell stack was consumed within approximately 90 seconds after nail penetration.
claims.quote: The results show a rapid propagation of thermal runaway after the initial nail penetration with the entire ﬁve cell string consumed within ∼90 s.

claims.claim: At 100% SOC without thermal management, the average peak temperature measured between cells 2 through 4 was 650°C.
claims.quote: The thermocouple-measured peak temperatures, when averaged across the thermocouples located between the cells (2–4), is 650 °C.

claims.claim: At 50% SOC, the short-circuit heat release and cell reactivity were not sufficient to trigger substantial thermal runaway or cascading propagation to neighboring cells.
claims.quote: At this low SOC, the short-circuit heat release and cell reactivity are not sufﬁcient to trigger a substantial thermal runaway and are well away from triggering thermal runaway in neighboring cells.

claims.claim: At 75% SOC, the short-circuited cell reached approximately 327°C, which caused failure in the second cell, but cascading propagation halted at the second cell with the thermocouple between cells 1 and 2 reaching only approximately 283°C.
claims.quote: Figure 4 (right) shows the response at 75% SOC, with the short-circuited cell reaching ∼327 °C. This was sufﬁcient to cause cell failure in the second cell of the stack as represented by voltage loss. However, the thermocouple between cells 1 and 2 only reached ∼283 °C and the cascading propagation halted at the second cell.

claims.claim: At 80% SOC, two out of four replicate tests showed full propagation and two showed no propagation, making 80% SOC the boundary between propagation and failure to propagate for this cell configuration.
claims.quote: For two cases propagation occurred as shown on the left of Fig. 5, but for two cases propagation failed to occur (right of Fig. 5). With two of four repeated tests giving propagation, this 80% SOC can be considered as the separation between propagation and failure to propagate.

claims.claim: At 80% SOC with full propagation, the maximum internal thermocouple temperatures averaged approximately 560°C, which is lower than the 650°C observed at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: The peak temperatures in the propagating cases were observed to be somewhat lower than those at 100% SOC, with maximum temperatures for each internal thermocouple averaging to ∼560 °C.

claims.claim: Increasing SOC from 80% to 100% decreases cell-crossing time, with cell-crossing occurring in 10 to 12 seconds at 100% SOC compared to 12 to 18 seconds at 80% SOC.
claims.quote: An increase in SOC (which is related to the heat release) leads to a faster cell-crossing time, which is expected as more energy is available to drive propagation. Cell-crossing occurs in 10 to 12 s for 100% SOC compared to 12 to 18 s for 80% SOC.

claims.claim: Adding 1.6 mm aluminum or copper plates between cells at 100% SOC limited propagation to only the second cell, with no further propagation beyond that.
claims.quote: At this thickness, only limited propagation to the nearest neighbor is observed. After the initial cell failure, the second cell in the string failed with a modest temperature rise and voltage loss. However, the kinetics of this failure are muted, and no further propagation is observed beyond the second cell.

claims.claim: Adding 0.8 mm aluminum or copper plates between cells at 100% SOC resulted in full propagation across all five cells, but propagation time was approximately doubled to about 200 seconds compared to approximately 90 seconds for the unmitigated 100% SOC case.
claims.quote: In both cases, the ﬁnal cell failure was observed ∼200 s after the initial cell failure. This is about twice the time required for the unmitigated 100% SOC cell stack to propagate across ﬁve cells in Fig. 2.

claims.claim: Thicker metal plates (3.2 mm aluminum and copper) completely mitigated thermal runaway propagation beyond the initiating cell.
claims.quote: Thicker plates were shown to completely mitigate the propagation.

claims.claim: The addition of metal plates significantly decreases cell-crossing time to generally faster than 10 seconds, making the instantaneous propagation velocity within a cell faster than in unmitigated stacks, despite the plates absorbing some thermal energy.
claims.quote: The addition of plates signiﬁcantly decreases the cell-crossing time despite the fact that the plates will absorb some of the thermal energy released. With metal plates between cells, the cell-crossing times are generally faster than 10 s indicating that there are aspects of the nearly mitigated case that can be more severe than unmitigated cell stacks.

claims.claim: When metal plates are added and propagation is not prevented, the longer gap-crossing time results in more heat distributed through the next cell before ignition, leading to faster propagation through that cell after ignition due to Arrhenius rate processes.
claims.quote: when the gap-crossing times increase, more heat spreads into the next cell while raising its peak temperature towards the critical temperature where thermal runaway begins... More energy is associated with faster heat release (Arrhenius reaction rates), thus increasing the velocity of the reaction front.

claims.claim: The average cell-to-cell thermal contact resistance was estimated at 12.3×10⁻³ m²·K·W⁻¹, while the average cell-to-plate contact resistance was 4.8×10⁻³ m²·K·W⁻¹, giving an equivalent cell-to-plate-to-cell resistance of approximately 9.6×10⁻³ m²·K·W⁻¹.
claims.quote: The average values for the cell-to-cell and cell-to-plate resistance are 12.3e-3 m2 K W−1 and 4.8e-3 m2 K W−1 contact respectively, but there are two cell-to-plate contacts between each cell with metal plates. The equivalent resistance for cell-to-plate-to-cell of 9.6e-3 m2 K W−1 (two times cell-to-plate) is on the same order as the cell-to-cell contact resistance.

claims.claim: The thermal resistance of the metal plates themselves is smaller than 1×10⁻⁵ m²·K·W⁻¹, which is much less than the contact resistances at cell-to-cell or cell-to-plate interfaces.
claims.quote: We note that these contact resistances are much greater than the equivalent thermal resistance L/km of the plates themselves which are smaller than 1e-5 m2 K W−1.

claims.claim: For the 100% SOC case without plates, the average energy absorbed by the next cell during gap-crossing time was 2.0 kJ, increasing to 2.6 kJ and 2.5 kJ when 0.8 mm aluminum and copper plates were added, respectively.
claims.quote: For the 100% SOC case, the average energy absorbed by the next cell during the gap-crossing time was 2.0 kJ... When the 0.8 mm plates were added, the average energy increased to 2.6 kJ and 2.5 kJ for the aluminum and copper plates, respectively.

claims.claim: During gap-crossing, 0.8 mm aluminum and copper plates absorbed approximately 3.2 kJ and 4.1 kJ respectively, roughly tripling the total heat that must be transferred to initiate thermal runaway in the next cell compared to the no-plate case (approximately 5.7 kJ versus 2.0 kJ).
claims.quote: During the gap-crossing the 0.8 mm aluminum and copper plates are estimated to absorb 3.2 kJ and 4.1 kJ, respectively... the amount of heat that must be transferred from the hot cell to start thermal runaway in the next cell is roughly tripled in the case with metal plates (∼2.5 kJ + 3.2 kJ = 5.7 kJ compared to 2 kJ without plates).

claims.claim: Cascading propagation is well correlated with the ratio of stored energy to system heat capacity (potential temperature rise ΔT), with full propagation occurring above approximately 663°C potential temperature rise and complete mitigation below approximately 584°C for the tested cell type and configuration.
claims.quote: When the experiments are sorted by this stored energy per heat capacity, two transitions in the propagation behavior are apparent: from the 80% SOC to the 1.6 mm Aluminum plates case where propagation is limited to the second cell and from the 1.6 mm Copper plates case to the 3.2 mm Aluminum plates case (at 75% SOC) where thermal runaway is mitigated.

claims.claim: The propagation limit correlated with potential temperature rise is specific to the tested cell type and configuration and cannot be directly extrapolated to other cell types or configurations.
claims.quote: We suggest this limiting DT is speciﬁc to the cell type and conﬁguration, but the general alignment of cascading propagation vs mitigation should be well correlated with this potential temperature rise, DT.

claims.claim: Separator failures can begin to occur around 130°C, but most polypropylene separators maintain structural integrity up to 165°C.
claims.quote: Separator failures can begin to occur around 130°C, but most systems employ polypropylene that maintains structural integrity up to 165 °C.

claims.claim: Adding thermally conductive plates as mitigation would constitute a significant increase in weight, cost, and volume if applied to a pack of hundreds of cells.
claims.quote: While adding thermally conductive plates has been shown to be effective in small packs, it would constitute a signiﬁcant increase in weight, cost and volume if applied to a pack of hundreds of cells.

claims.claim: In the baseline (air-cooled) nail penetration test, all cell thermocouples rapidly exceeded 1000 °C within the first 20 seconds after nail penetration of Cell #1.
claims.quote: The temperature traces in the FIGURE 3 showed that all cell thermocouples rapidly exceeded 1000 °C within the first 20 seconds.

claims.claim: In the baseline (air-cooled) nail penetration test, peak temperatures exceeded the thermocouple measurement range of 1250 °C, with some readings reaching as high as 2200 °C, and the module voltage dropped abruptly to 0 V within a few seconds.
claims.quote: Peak temperatures recorded exceeded the measurement range of the thermocouples (i.e., >1250 °C), with some readings reaching as high as 2200 °C... Voltage readings from FIGURE 5 (A) revealed that the module voltage dropped abruptly to 0 V within a few seconds, confirming total electrical failure of the mini-module.

claims.claim: Under active immersion cooling at 2 LPM and 25 °C fluid temperature, only four thermocouples exceeded 200 °C after nail penetration of Cell #1, and the highest recorded temperature remained below 400 °C, with most cell surface temperatures dropping below 100 °C within 60–90 seconds.
claims.quote: temperature data revealed that only four thermocouples—primarily near Cell #1—exceeded 200 °C. The highest recorded temperature remained below 400 °C. The continuously circulating dielectric fluid rapidly dissipated the heat across the module, and most cell surface temperatures dropped below 100 °C within 60–90 seconds.

claims.claim: Under immersion cooling, the coolant flow rate dropped to near zero after two minutes due to vaporization of the bulk of the coolant following nail penetration.
claims.quote: The flow rate dropped to near zero after two minutes due to vaporization of the bulk of the coolant, as evident in the flow rate curve – observed in FIGURE 5 (B).

claims.claim: In the immersion-cooled test, two of seven cells (Cell #2 and Cell #6) retained near-nominal voltages of 4.1 V and 4.2 V respectively after nail penetration, while no cells retained measurable voltage in the baseline case; the mini-module weight loss was approximately 24.4 grams in the immersion-cooled case versus more than 260 grams in the baseline case.
claims.quote: Post-test disassembly revealed two cells (Cell #2 and Cell #6) with intact casing and retained voltages of 4.1 V and 4.2 V, respectively... TABLE 1 also showed the weight of the mini-module did not change much for the immersion cooled test after the test – around ~24.4 grams, whereas that for the baseline test reduced by more than 260 grams.

claims.claim: During overcharge abuse tests of LTO cells at 100% SOC and 40A (4C), the use of synthetic ester and silicone oil as immersion fluids resulted in strong explosions and severe flame generation from both the battery and the liquids, making them detrimental from a safety perspective.
claims.quote: the use of synthetic ester and silicone oil proved to be detrimental from a safety perspective: in both cases, after the initial overcharge, cell bottom loss, and abundant venting gas release, a strong explosion and severe flame generation from both the battery and the liquids were recorded

claims.claim: During overcharge abuse tests, PFPE170 immersion suppressed battery flames entirely, while PFPE270 only prevented flame propagation but still allowed a small flame on the battery; neither fluorinated fluid has a flash point or fire point.
claims.quote: The tested fluorinated fluids, which have neither a flash point nor fire point, were effective in preventing the propagation of battery flames in the case of PFPE270, and even in suppressing battery flames in the case of PFPE 170.

claims.claim: In single-cell overcharge tests at 100% SOC and 40A, all dielectric fluids delayed thermal runaway onset compared to open-air conditions, with silicone oil providing the largest delay followed by PFPE170, due to enhanced thermal exchange properties of the fluids compared to air.
claims.quote: The thermal runaway onset of the cell was delayed by varying time intervals depending on the fluid adopted, with silicon oil providing the largest delay, followed by PFPE 170. These delays were supported by the enhanced thermal exchange properties of the fluids compared to air.

claims.claim: In the battery pack configuration test, adjacent cells immersed in PFPE170 reached maximum surface temperatures of 62.5°C and 57°C, compared to 160.7°C and 92.3°C for adjacent cells in open-air conditions, demonstrating PFPE170's effectiveness in mitigating thermal propagation to neighboring cells.
claims.quote: In the open-air test, the maximum temperatures recorded on the two adjacent cells were 160.7 °C (T4) and 92.3 °C (T8), respectively. In contrast, during the second test with the cells immersed in PFPE170, the temperatures remained significantly lower and more uniform, being 62.5 °C (T2) and 57 °C (T8).

claims.claim: In single-cell overcharge tests, PFPE170 delayed the time to maximum voltage by 107 s relative to open-air conditions, which was the largest delay among the four fluids tested; silicone oil delayed by 103 s, synthetic ester by 77 s, and PFPE270 by 61 s.
claims.quote: PFPE 170 exhibited the most significant delay of 107 s, followed by silicone oil (103 s), synthetic ester (77 s), and PFPE 270 (61 s).

claims.claim: The minimum cut-off Reynolds number necessary for avoiding thermal runaway was 32 for NCA88 and 234 for NCA91 under forced air convection cooling.
claims.quote: the minimum Recut-off values necessary for avoiding TR were 32 for NCA88 and 234 for NCA91

claims.claim: To avoid thermal runaway in ASSBs, a minimum cut-off Reynolds number of 5000 to 10,000 is required, which is substantially higher than that required for NCA-based LIBs.
claims.quote: to avoid TR in ASSBs, a minimum Recut-off of 5000 ~ 10,000 is required

claims.claim: A 3% increase in nickel content from NCA88 to NCA91 increases the minimum required air velocity for effective battery cooling from 10 cm/s to 73 cm/s, irrespective of SOC.
claims.quote: a minimum air velocity required varied between 10 cm/s to 73 cm/s with an increase of 3 % of nickel content (NCA 88 to NCA91) in cathode composition

claims.claim: For NCA/SCN batteries with nickel content in the range of 85% to 95%, thermal runaway occurs at SOC greater than or equal to 50% irrespective of heating rate.
claims.quote: for NCA/SCN batteries with high range of nickel content (85 % ~ 95 %), the TR occurs at SOC ≥ 50 % irrespective of the heating rate

claims.claim: The critical dimensionless energy parameter delta_critical for NCA88/SCN is 5.75 at 100% SOC, and any value above 5.75 may lead to thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the present theory suggests 5.75 as a critical value of dimensionless energy parameter for NCA88/SCN at 100 % SOC. Any value above 5.75 may lead to TR in a cell

claims.claim: In open space, TRP is not self-sustainable; when jelly rolls of cells eject out completely, the remaining shell cannot transfer sufficient heat to trigger TR in following cells, and only 8 of 15 cells entered TR while the rest survived.
claims.quote: the jelly rolls of cell #6, #7 and #8 in row 2 and 3 almost all ejected out. The temperature of the empty cells with only shells cooled down quickly, thus hard to transfer heat to the following cells. Therefore, only 8 cells of the cluster fell into TR while the rest 7 cells survived.

claims.claim: In the enclosed cluster, all 15 cells entered TR within 103 seconds, while in open space TRP lasted approximately 1033 seconds for 15 cells, indicating TRP in confined space was approximately 17 times faster than in open space.
claims.quote: In the enclosed cluster, all 15 cells went into TR and caught fire within a short period of 103 s, starting from 450 s and ending at 553 s... the heat is dissipated to the environment so the TRP may not be self-sustainable or lasted about 17 times longer than that in the former condition.

claims.claim: The normalized heat release per cell in confined space (71.4 kJ/cell) was higher than in open space (48.8 kJ/cell), indicating that the cascading fire in confined space enhanced the combustion of each cell.
claims.quote: The normalized heat release per cell in the open space and confined space were 48.8 kJ/cell and 71.4 kJ/cell, respectively, indicating that the cascading fire in confined space enhanced the combustion of the cell to some extent.

claims.claim: When the ambient temperature was lower than 17.4°C, TR could only propagate to the first row or adjacent cell even in a confined cluster with no gaps between cells.
claims.quote: when the temperature was lower than 17.4 ◦C, only the triggered cell #2 or its adjacent cell #1 fell into TR even for the case without gaps between cells (the most dangerous condition).

claims.claim: Row-to-row TRP speed in air was 0.7 row per second, which was 9 times faster than in nitrogen, indicating a significant acceleration effect due to flaming combustion.
claims.quote: the row-to-row TRP speed in the air was 0.7 row per second, which was 9 times faster than that in the nitrogen, indicating significant acceleration effect due to flaming combustion.

claims.claim: Without LN intervention, TR in the central module caused the upper adjacent module (M2) to reach a peak temperature of 131.2°C at 3100 s, driven by vertical flame ejection and buoyancy-induced convective flow.
claims.quote: M2 experienced the most significant temperature rise, with a peak of 131.2 ◦C reached at 3100 s, marking the end of Stage I and the onset of Stage II. This intense heating is attributed to the vertical flame ejection and hot gas release during TR, which are driven upward by buoyancy forces and expelled through the battery's safety valve.

claims.claim: Without LN intervention, the bottom adjacent module (M3) reached a peak temperature of 63.17°C, while lateral modules (M4 and M5) increased by only 27.8°C, indicating that vertical propagation dominates over lateral propagation in the early TRP stage.
claims.quote: M3 (bottom) showed moderate heating, peaking at 63.17 ◦C, and M4 and M5 (left and right sides) experienced only minor temperature changes, increasing by 27.8 ◦C at 2000 s and then stabilizing. This difference is primarily due to the lack of direct flame contact and structural separation between side modules... lateral propagation in tightly packed prismatic battery modules is often limited to radiative heat transfer.

claims.claim: After LN injection (11 kg), the temperatures of all adjacent modules dropped to below room temperature, and the TR module fire duration was reduced to 5150 s, approximately one-third of the 15,700 s duration observed without LN.
claims.quote: the fire progress of TR module with LN injection features a significantly shortened duration of only 5150 s, approximately one-third of the original duration.

claims.claim: As LN injection mass increased from 11 kg to 13 kg to 18 kg, cooling rates increased from 0.05°C/s to 0.11°C/s to 0.97°C/s, while heat absorption efficiency decreased from 31.7 J/g to 27.4 J/g to 17.34 J/g, demonstrating a trade-off between cooling rate and LN utilization efficiency.
claims.quote: the maximum cooling rates under the three LN injection masses (11 kg, 13 kg, and 18 kg) reach 0.05 ◦C/s, 0.11 ◦C/s, and 0.97 ◦C/s, respectively... As LN injection mass increases, φ reduces from 31.7 J/g to 17.34 J/g, illustrating the decrease of LN utilization efficiency.

claims.claim: Under the 18 kg LN injection condition, the peak temperature of adjacent modules showed a slight increase compared to 11 kg injection (34.2°C vs 31.2°C), attributed to convective turbulence and localized pressure disturbances caused by rapid vaporization of the larger LN quantity.
claims.quote: The peak temperature of three operating conditions reaches 31.2 ◦C, 31.3 ◦C, and 34.2 ◦C. The slight increase in peak temperature under the highest LN injection mass may be attributed to transient thermal and fluid dynamic disturbances caused by the rapid vaporization of LN upon contact with high-temperature surfaces.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway (TR) can be prevented by applying water mist (WM) before the critical temperature is reached, and the critical suppression temperature (Tc) is at least 20 °C lower than the TR onset temperature for SOC of 50%–100%.
claims.quote: TR can be controlled when the WM is released before the critical temperature is reached; the threshold temperature is at least 20 °C lower than the temperature of the TR onset.

claims.claim: The critical water mist suppression temperature decreases from 256.8 ± 3.5 °C at 25% SOC to 186.5 ± 0.7 °C at 100% SOC for 18650 LIBs, indicating greater suppression difficulty at higher SOC.
claims.quote: The threshold values range from 256.8 ± 3.5 °C to 186.5 ± 0.7 °C for SOC of 25%–100%; Tc is at least 20 °C lower than the TR onset temperature of the corresponding SOC.

claims.claim: At 25% SOC, the critical WM cooling rate is 1.87 times the critical heating rate, and this ratio increases to 4.98 at 100% SOC, indicating increasing suppression difficulty with increasing SOC.
claims.quote: At 25% SOC, the critical WM cooling rate is 1.87 times that of the critical heating rate. The value increases to 4.98 at 100% SOC, thereby indicating the increasing suppression diﬃculty with increasing SOC.

claims.claim: Above the critical temperature, TR is unstoppable during WM application; however, the maximum surface temperature can be controlled at values approximately 300 °C lower than those of cases without WM.
claims.quote: Above the critical temperature, the TR is unstoppable. However, the maximal surface temperature can be controlled at values approximately 300 °C lower than those of the cases without WM.

claims.claim: Under continuous heating after WM suppression, TR is totally prevented at 25% SOC, but the thermal hazard remains for higher SOC with TR onset times approximately tripled at 50% SOC (approximately 30 min) and delayed by approximately 21 min at 100% SOC compared to cases without WM.
claims.quote: TR is totally prevented at 25% SOC. In addition, the thermal hazard is mitigated for higher SOC. The onset time of TR is multiplied by approximately three (approximately 30 min at 50% SOC). In addition, the delaying eﬀect decreases with increasing SOC. However, it still lasts approximately 21 min at 100% SOC.

claims.claim: During water mist application on a lithium-ion battery module, the maximum cooling rate of a single LIB exceeded 100 K/s and the surface temperature decreased to 373.15 K in a few seconds.
claims.quote: During WM application, the maximum cooling rate of LIB exceeded 100 K s−1 and the surface temperature decreased to 373.15 K in a few seconds.

claims.claim: The water mist cooling power for a single 18650 battery, calculated assuming all droplets evaporate immediately on the battery surface above 373.15 K, was 40.71 W.
claims.quote: For an equal WM flow field, the calculated cooling power is 40.71 W.

claims.claim: The battery heating power at the beginning of thermal runaway was 51.16 W and reached a maximum of 2448.20 W, which was over 60 times higher than the water mist cooling power of 40.71 W, making the water mist cooling effect negligible during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Ph was 51.16 W at the beginning of TR, and reached a maximum of 2448.20 W, which was over 60 times as high as Pb. Hence, the WM cooling effect was negligible during TR, and the maximum battery temperature could still exceed 850 K.

claims.claim: When the battery pre-heated temperature (Tp) exceeded the critical control temperature Tc1, the battery temperature exceeded Tc2 due to rapid heat transfer from the adjacent battery and thermal runaway occurred during water mist application.
claims.quote: for Tp > Tc1, the temperature exceeds Tc2 due to the rapid heating effect of battery #1 and TR occurs in battery #2 during WM application, which can be used to explain the TR of battery #4 in tests 6 and 7.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway propagation in a battery module could be prevented with a water consumption of 1.95 × 10−4 kg Wh−1, which was the lowest among the compared control strategies.
claims.quote: Consumption of 1.95 × 10−4 kg Wh−1 water was enough to prevent TR propagation, which was the lightest among the compared cases.

claims.claim: The proportion of combustion heat release increased with increasing SOC, but heat accumulation dominated the thermal runaway propagation process in the well-ventilated experimental setup where thermal feedback on the battery module was negligible.
claims.quote: The proportion of the CHR increased with increasing SOC, indicating a much more energetic TR propagation behavior at higher SOC... Compared with the CHR, the HA curve is relatively gentle... it is reasonable to deduce that the HA dominated the TR propagation process in this study.

claims.claim: The average thermal runaway propagation time intervals between neighboring batteries in row 1 decreased with increasing SOC, indicating increased thermal risk at higher SOC, while row 2 propagation time intervals remained at approximately 30 s regardless of SOC.
claims.quote: The time intervals decreased with increasing SOC in row 1, indicating an increased thermal risk at higher SOC. In row 2, the TR propagation time interval remained at approximately 30 s, which is much lower than that in row 1.

claims.claim: The solid electrolyte interphase film on the anode begins to decompose at 70–120 °C during thermal abuse of lithium-ion batteries.
claims.quote: The solid electrolyte interphase film on the anode begins to decompose at 70–120 ◦C first

claims.claim: Internal short circuits occur after shrinkage of the separator, resulting in a thermal runaway accident manifesting as a slow to rapid heating transition.
claims.quote: the internal short circuits (ISC) occur after the shrinkage of the separator, resulting in a TR accident and manifesting as a slow to rapid heating transition

claims.claim: In a 156 Ah LIB containing 4 jelly rolls, the temperature difference between the inside and outside exceeded 400 °C during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Wang et al. [23] studied the internal temperature variation characteristics of a 156Ah LIB containing 4 jelly rolls and found that the temperature difference between the inside and outside exceeded 400 ◦C

claims.claim: Combustion of a 300 Ah LIB containing 2 jelly rolls under external heating lasted for nearly 14 minutes.
claims.quote: Mao et al. [24] studied the combustion behavior of 300Ah LIBs containing 2 jelly rolls under external heating and found that the combustion can last for nearly 14 min

claims.claim: A prolonged combustion process in large-capacity LIBs brings a greater risk of disaster propagation.
claims.quote: a prolonged combustion process brings a greater risk of disaster propagation

claims.claim: The SiO2 aerogel composite reinforced with fiberglass has an ultra-low thermal conductivity of 0.021 W/m·K.
claims.quote: The composite combines ultra-low thermal conductivity (0.021 W/m⋅K), high thermal stability (mass loss <4 % only above 300 ◦C), and robust hydrophobicity (contact angle >135◦).

claims.claim: A 2 mm aerogel barrier attenuates approximately 50% of transmitted heat and completely prevents thermal runaway propagation.
claims.quote: a 2 mm barrier attenuating approximately 50 % of transmitted heat and completely preventing TRP.

claims.claim: Increasing aerogel barrier thickness linearly extended the delay before adjacent batteries experienced thermal runaway propagation and markedly reduced the severity of propagation.
claims.quote: Systematic testing demonstrated that increasing barrier thickness linearly extended the delay before adjacent batteries experienced TRP and markedly reduced the severity of propagation.

claims.claim: At 0% SOC, a 1 mm aerogel barrier is sufficient to prevent thermal runaway propagation, whereas at 50%, 75%, and 100% SOC, a 2 mm barrier is required to stop TRP.
claims.quote: At 0 % SOC a 1 mm barrier layer suffices to prevent propagation, while at 50 %, 75 % and 100 % SOC cells need a 2 mm barrier layer to stop TRP.

claims.claim: Compared to 0% SOC, peak temperatures of Battery 2# under full contact increased by 103.98%, 115.65%, and 180.93% at 50%, 75%, and 100% SOC, respectively.
claims.quote: compared to 0 % SOC, peak temperatures increase by 103.98 %, 115.65 %, and 180.93 % at 50 %, 75 %, and 100 % SOC, respectively.

claims.claim: The time interval between thermal runaway events for Battery 1# and Battery 2# decreases with increasing SOC under full contact conditions, measuring 75 s, 46 s, 29 s, and 26 s at 0%, 50%, 75%, and 100% SOC, respectively.
claims.quote: The time intervals between TR occurrences for Battery 1# and Battery 2# are 75 s, 46 s, 29 s, and 26 s, respectively. The results demonstrate that, with increasing SOC, the time interval between TR events for Battery 1# and Battery 2# decreases.

claims.claim: The interval between runaway events in the two batteries increases from 26 s under full contact to 247 s with a 0.5 mm barrier and to 394 s with a 1 mm barrier at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: The interval between runaway events in the two batteries increases from 26 s under full contact to 247 s with a 0.5 mm barrier and to 394 s with a 1 mm barrier, demonstrating that thicker barriers more effectively impede heat transfer.

claims.claim: The activation energy for thermal runaway decreases with increasing SOC: 4.25 eV at 0% SOC, 2.61 eV at 50% SOC, 2.54 eV at 75% SOC, and 2.34 eV at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: The results indicate that the Ea for 0 % SOC, 50 % SOC, 75 % SOC, and 100 % SOC batteries are 4.25 eV, 2.61 eV, 2.54 eV, and 2.34 eV, respectively.

claims.claim: Mass loss of Battery 2# after thermal runaway under full contact increases with SOC: 4.0 g (11.83%) at 0% SOC, 6.7 g (19.82%) at 50% SOC, 7.7 g (22.58%) at 75% SOC, and 10 g (29.24%) at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: The mass loss after TR for the 0 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 % SOC batteries are 4.0 g, 6.7 g, 7.7 g, and 10 g, respectively; the mass loss rates are 11.83 %, 19.82 %, 22.58 %, and 29.24 %.

claims.claim: For 100% SOC cells, heat transferred to Battery 2# before thermal runaway decreases from 5.9 kJ to 5.4, 4.3, and 1.8 kJ as aerogel blanket thickness increases from 0 to 0.5, 1, and 2 mm.
claims.quote: For the 100 % SOC cells, heat transfer before TR decreases from 5.9 kJ to 5.4, 4.3 and 1.8 kJ as blanket thickness increases.

claims.claim: The aerogel composite exhibits significant mass loss only above 300°C, with total mass loss ranging from 3.3% to 3.6%, and retains ≥96% mass at 1000°C.
claims.quote: the mass of the sample begins to change significantly only after 300 ◦C, with a total mass loss ranging from 3.3 % to 3.6 %. ... After 1000 ◦C, the sample's mass remains stable at ≥96 %.

claims.claim: With a 2 mm aerogel barrier, the risk level for all SOC packs is classified as Level IV (safe) according to the risk matrix analysis.
claims.quote: with a 2 mm inhibition thickness, the risk level for all packs is classified as level IV (safe), indicating that aerogel blankets are excellent insulating inhibition materials, and a 2 mm thickness provides the best barrier effect.

claims.claim: In the absence of a barrier, heat exchange between batteries occurs primarily through conduction and flame radiation, whereas with a barrier in place, thermal radiation dominates heat transfer.
claims.quote: In the absence of a barrier, the batteries exchange heat primarily through conduction and flame radiation. With a barrier in place, thermal radiation dominates heat transfer.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature rise rate of Battery 2# at 100% SOC increases from 63°C/s under full contact to 144.6°C/s with a 0.5 mm barrier and 117.25°C/s with a 1 mm barrier, but does not trigger runaway with a 2 mm barrier.
claims.quote: Battery 2# still experiences TR, reaching peak rise rates of 63 ◦C/s, 144.6 ◦C/s and 117.25 ◦C/s, respectively. With a 2 mm barrier in place, Battery 2# exhibits only a modest temperature increase during TR of Battery 1# and does not cross the runaway threshold.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway (TR) is prevented by applying liquid nitrogen (LN) before the battery surface temperature reaches the critical TR suppression temperature (Ttr,cs).
claims.quote: TR can be prevented by applying LN before the critical TR suppression temperature is reached (Ttr,cs). The threshold values range from 270.2 ± 0.7 ◦C to 170.2 ± 1.1 ◦C for SOC of 25%–100%.

claims.claim: When the battery surface temperature (Tw) exceeds the critical TR suppression temperature (Ttr,cs), thermal runaway is unavoidable even during the application of liquid nitrogen.
claims.quote: When the surface temperature of the battery (Tw) exceeds Ttr,cs, TR is unavoidable even during the application of LN.

claims.claim: The delaying effect of liquid nitrogen on thermal runaway onset time is weakened as the battery surface temperature (Tw) increases toward the triggering temperature (Ttr).
claims.quote: for all SOC, when Tw is closer to the triggering temperature (Ttr), the delayed time is shorter, indicating the delaying effect of LN is weakened with increasing Tw.

claims.claim: Only 29.3 g of liquid nitrogen reduces the temperature of a 9.24 Wh (100% SOC) 18650 battery from approximately 700°C to less than 100°C within 80 seconds.
claims.quote: only 29.3 g of LN can reduce the temperature of 9.24 Wh (2.2 mAh × 4.2 V) battery from about 700 ◦C to no more than 100 ◦C within 80s, indicating that a very small of liquid nitrogen can control the triggering battery to a relatively safe level.

claims.claim: The effective heat utilization efficiency (εe) of liquid nitrogen decreases from 80% to 37.8% as the mass of LN sprayed on the battery (mLN) increases from 5.86 g to 29.3 g, indicating cooling efficiency decreases with increasing LN application duration.
claims.quote: The value of εe decreases from 80% to 37.8% when mLN rises from 5.86 to 29.3 g, implying that the cooling efficiency of LN gradually decreases with increasing mLN.

claims.claim: The calculated average cooling rate (cave) of liquid nitrogen decreases from 10.3°C/s to 2.84°C/s as the battery surface temperature (Tw) decreases from 800°C to 0°C, indicating that the cooling effect of LN weakens with decreasing Tw.
claims.quote: the cave decreases from 10.3 ◦C/s to 2.84 ◦C/s when the surface temperature reduces from 800 ◦C to 0 ◦C, indicating that the cooling effect of LN is weakened with decreasing Tw.

claims.claim: When the thermal conductivity of the composite phase change firewall substrate increases from 0.036 W/(m·K) to 0.046 W/(m·K), the peak temperature of the front surface of Cell2 rises from 136°C to 175°C (a relative increase of 22.2%), but thermal runaway zero-spreading is still achieved.
claims.quote: When the thermal conductivity of the base material increases from 0.036 W/(m⋅K) to 0.046 W/(m⋅K), although the peak temperature on the front surface of Cell2 rises from 136 ◦C to 175 ◦C (with a relative increase of 22.2 %), the module still maintains the zero-propagation effect of thermal runaway.

claims.claim: When the thermal conductivity of the composite phase change firewall substrate increases to 0.051 W/(m·K), Cell2 triggers thermal runaway, indicating a critical threshold between 0.046 W/(m·K) and 0.051 W/(m·K) above which the insulation layer fails.
claims.quote: When the thermal conductivity of the base material further increases to 0.051 W/(m⋅K), Cell2 triggers thermal runaway, which then propagates within the module. This indicates that there is a critical value of the base material's thermal conductivity between 0.046 and 0.051 W/(m⋅K): when the thermal conductivity is lower than this critical value, the composite phase change thermal insulation layer can effectively prevent the propagation of thermal runaway; once it exceeds this critical value, the insulation layer fails, and Cell 2 will trigger thermal runaway.

claims.claim: When the PCM adsorption capacity in the composite phase change firewall decreases from 100% to 50%, the peak temperature of the front surface of Cell2 rises from 137°C to 156°C (a relative increase of 13.9%), but thermal runaway zero-spreading is still achieved.
claims.quote: When the PCM adsorption capacity in the phase change thermal insulation layer decreases from 100 % to 50 %, although the peak temperature on the front surface of Cell2 rises from 137 ◦C to 156 ◦C (with a relative increase of 13.9 %), the zero-propagation effect of thermal runaway is still achieved.

claims.claim: When the PCM adsorption capacity in the composite phase change firewall decreases to 40%, Cell2 triggers thermal runaway, indicating a critical threshold between 40% and 50% below which the firewall no longer prevents thermal runaway propagation.
claims.quote: When the PCM adsorption capacity further decreases to 40 %, Cell2 triggers thermal runaway. This indicates that there is a critical value of PCM adsorption capacity between 40 % and 50 %: when the PCM adsorption capacity is lower than this critical value, the composite phase change thermal insulation layer no longer has the effect of preventing the propagation of thermal runaway.

claims.claim: With increasing substrate thermal conductivity of the composite phase change firewall, the TR propagation time interval D1,2 decreases in a gradient, with D1,2 shrinking from 2900 s at 0.051 W/(m·K) to 2372 s at 0.056 W/(m·K).
claims.quote: When the thermal conductivity of the substrate increases to 0.051 W/(m⋅K), the TR propagation time interval D1,2 of the first and second batteries is 2900 s. When the thermal conductivity of the substrate further increases to 0.056 W/(m⋅K), D1,2 shrinks to 2372 s. With the increase of the thermal conductivity of the substrate material, D1,2 decreases in a gradient.

claims.claim: Water mist containing Tween 20 achieved a heat dissipation of 12.46 ± 0.20 kJ, which is 23.6% higher than the test with pure water.
claims.quote: The heat dissipation of Tween 20 is 12.46 ± 0.20 kJ, which exhibits the best cooling effect, and it is increased by 23.6% compared with the test with pure water.

claims.claim: The maximum heat dissipation rate of water mist is 308.38 W, which is only 3.54% of the heat generation rate during thermal runaway, making it insufficient to restrain the rapid temperature rise of the cell during TR.
claims.quote: Thus, the max Pw is about 308.38 W, which is only 3.54% of Pg. Hence, it is hard for water mist to restrain the rapid temperature rise of the cell during TR.

claims.claim: Water mist containing FS-51 inhibited heat transfer compared to pure water, with the lowest average cooling rate of T2 at 5.47 ± 0.33 °C/s versus 6.60 ± 0.37 °C/s for pure water.
claims.quote: Different from other additives, FS-51 has the lowest cooling rates of T1 and T2, which indicates that water mist containing FS-51 could not provide a better cooling effect, but would inhibit the cooling of pure water.

claims.claim: The binary mixture of Tween 20 and 1-heptanol at any volume ratio did not exhibit better cooling performance than either single additive alone.
claims.quote: Hence, no matter what the volume ratio was mixed, the binary mixture of Tween 20 and 1-heptanol did not exhibit a better cooling performance than a single additive.

claims.claim: FS-51, despite having the lowest surface tension (15.0 mN/m), inhibited cooling due to its highest foamability with an expansion ratio of 49.4%, which was 20.6% higher than that of Tween 20.
claims.quote: FS-51 showed the best performance among other additives, and its expansion ratio was 49.4%, which was 20.6% higher than that of Tween 20... The FS-51 had a low surface tension, which facilitated cooling, but the ultimate effect was to inhibit cooling due to foamability.

claims.claim: The heat dissipation of HFC-227ea, C6F12O, and water spray during fire extinguishing tests is 24.8 kJ, 111 kJ, and 459.8 kJ, respectively.
claims.quote: The heat dissipation of HFC-227ea, C6F12O and water spray in fire extinguishing test is 24.8 kJ, 111 kJ and 459.8 kJ, respectively.

claims.claim: Water spray prevented thermal runaway propagation in the LIB module, while HFC-227ea and C6F12O could not prevent thermal runaway propagation despite extinguishing open flames.
claims.quote: HFC-227ea and C6F12O can not prevent thermal runaway propagation. Among them, the suppression effect of HFC-227ea is limited, while C6F12O can greatly prolong the thermal runaway propagation time. Water spray can prevent thermal runaway propagation in LIBs module.

claims.claim: C6F12O application prolonged the thermal runaway propagation time to 256 s compared to 159 s without any extinguishing agent.
claims.quote: The thermal runaway propagation time was 256 s, which is longer than the preliminary test

claims.claim: Without extinguishing agent, thermal runaway propagation occurred when heat transfer between cells reached approximately 16 kJ.
claims.quote: Without extinguishing agent, most of the residual heat was used to heat the cell itself, and thermal runaway propagation can occur when the heat transfer between cells reach around 16 kJ.

claims.claim: A 0.5 mm aerogel insulation panel could not prevent thermal runaway propagation in the battery module, making auxiliary cooling measures essential.
claims.quote: A 0.5 mm aerogel thermal insulation panel cannot prevent thermal runaway propagation in the battery module. Therefore, it is essential to take auxiliary cooling measures suppress thermal runaway propagation.

claims.claim: Zirconia ceramic maintains more than 85% reflectance across the infrared spectrum from 2.5 µm to 15.4 µm, with an average reflectance of 96.8% in the 3–4 µm peak wavelength region corresponding to 400–600°C.
claims.quote: Fig. 2(a) shows the infrared reflectance of zirconia ceramic in the range of 2.5 µm - 15.4 µm, it can be seen that it maintains more than 85 % of the reflectance in the infrared spectrum. According to Wien's displacement law, 3–4 µm is the peak wavelength in the main region of 400–600 ℃. The average reflectance of zirconia ceramic in this region is 96.8 %.

claims.claim: The thermal conductivity of zirconia ceramics is 3 W/m·K at room temperature, while the thermal conductivity of aerogel felt is 0.018 W/m·K at room temperature.
claims.quote: the thermal conductivity of zirconia ceramics is 3 W/m⋅K at room temperature, which is not a low conductivity, and the thermal conductivity of the aerogel felt is 0.018 W/m⋅K at room temperature.

claims.claim: In confined space, the maximum surface temperature of the 100% SOC cell increased from 657.1°C in open space to 725.5°C, and the maximum surface temperature of the 50% SOC cell increased from 519.3°C to 575.8°C, with the increase being more pronounced for the 100% SOC cell.
claims.quote: the maximum surface temperature of the 100 % SOC cell increases from 657.1 ◦C to 725.5 ◦C in confined space. Similarly, the maximum surface temperature of the 50 % SOC cell rises from 519.3 ◦C to 575.8 ◦C. Notably, the increase in maximum temperature is more pronounced for the 100 % SOC cell compared to the 50 % SOC cell.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature rise rate of the 100% SOC cell module increased from 81.83°C/s in open space to 102.42°C/s in confined space, and the 50% SOC cell module's maximum temperature rise rate increased from 34.57°C/s to 41.75°C/s in confined space.
claims.quote: the maximum temperature rise rate of the 100 % SOC cell module increases from 81.83 ◦C/s in open space to 102.42 ◦C/s in confined space. Similarly, the 50 % SOC cell module's maximum temperature rise rate rises from 34.57 ◦C/s to 41.75 ◦C/s.

claims.claim: The combination of 2 mm zirconia and 1 mm aerogel felt achieved TRP delay times of 815 s and 179 s between successive cells, which was more effective than 3 mm zirconia alone (delay times of 402 s and 170 s) and 1 mm zirconia with 2 mm aerogel felt (delay times of 459 s and 351 s) at 100% SOC in confined space.
claims.quote: The combination of 2 mm zirconia and 1 mm aerogel felt significantly suppress TRP, with delay times of 815 s and 179 s, meeting relevant standards... From the experimental observations, although 3 mm zirconia delays the propagation of TR in the cell, the delay time is insufficient, being only 402 s and 170 s for the first two cells, respectively... For the 1 mm zirconia and 2 mm aerogel felt combination... the suppression times are 459 s and 351 s, respectively, which are not as effective as the 2 mm zirconia and 1 mm aerogel felt combination.

claims.claim: In a 40 Ah LiFePO4 battery module without any suppression, the TRP time intervals between batteries 1-2, 2-3, and 3-4 were 497 s, 639 s, and 266 s respectively, indicating that TRP accelerated in later stages due to heat accumulation.
claims.quote: Calculations reveal that the ΔtTRP(n-n+1) between the four batteries were 497 s, 639 s, and 266 s, respectively, while the Δt(TRP-inside)-n for each battery were 12 s, 16 s, 16 s, and 35 s. This indicates that as TRP progresses, the heat transfer between batteries accelerated due to increased thermal heat generation in the system.

claims.claim: The back surface temperatures of batteries 1–4 at the time of their respective TR events were 82°C, 96.8°C, 113.8°C, and 129.4°C, indicating a progressive preheating effect as TRP advanced through the module.
claims.quote: The variation can be attributed to the preheating effect caused by the TR of the first two batteries, as corroborated by the back surface temperatures of the four batteries during TR: 82°C, 96.8°C, 113.8°C, and 129.4°C, respectively.

claims.claim: Adding insulation material alone reduced heat received by battery 2 in Stage I from 48.81 kJ to 13.69 kJ (71.96% reduction) and in Stage II from 86.16 kJ to 59.95 kJ (30.42% reduction), demonstrating that insulation material was more effective in Stage I than Stage II.
claims.quote: Compared to Test 1, the addition of insulation material significantly reduced the heat received by battery 2 in both stages, from 48.81 kJ and 86.16 kJ to 13.69 kJ and 59.95 kJ, respectively. The percentages of heat reduction by the insulation material in the two stages were 71.96 % and 30.42 %, respectively, demonstrating that the insulation material was more effective in suppressing heat transfer during Stage I.

claims.claim: Adding liquid cooling (LC) to insulation reduced total heat absorbed by battery 2 from 73.64 kJ (insulation only) to 43.74 kJ, while adding CPCMs reduced it to 63.76 kJ, indicating LC was more effective than CPCMs at reducing total heat absorption.
claims.quote: The addition of CPCMs did not significantly affect T2,fmax and T2,bmax. However, it reduced the total heat absorbed by battery 2 (QTotal) from 73.64 kJ to 63.76 kJ. Moreover, introducing LC further reduced QTotal to 43.74 kJ. These results indicate that both CPCMs and LC reduced the heat absorbed by battery 2, providing enhanced protection, with LC being more effective than CPCMs.

claims.claim: The combination of insulation material and LC achieved heat suppression ratios of approximately 65–72% across both 40 Ah and 280 Ah battery modules, indicating stable and consistent suppression performance regardless of battery capacity.
claims.quote: the heat suppression ratios were 70.64 % in stage I and 71.84 % in stage II for test 4, and 65.87 % and 65.08 % for test 5, respectively. These results indicate that the heat suppression effect of the insulation and LC combination was remarkably stable and consistent across battery modules of different capacities.

claims.claim: When liquid nitrogen (LN) is sprayed on an 18650 LIB in open space, the cell surface temperature can be rapidly reduced to −170°C, and the voltage drops below zero simultaneously.
claims.quote: LN holds remarkable cooling effect, which can sharply reduce the face temperature of battery to −170°C. At the same time, the voltage drops below zero fora short time.

claims.claim: After LN spraying is stopped, the battery voltage returns to normal levels when the surrounding temperature reverts to indoor temperature, indicating LN does not damage the cell structure.
claims.quote: when the surrounding temperature reverts to indoor temperature, the voltage of the battery can return to normal levels. LN showed significant cooling effects without damaging the cell structure.

claims.claim: The average cooling rate of LIB surface temperature decreases with increasing heating power when LN is sprayed vertically for 100 s at 100% SOC; at 10 W, 15 W, 20 W, and 25 W, the average cooling rates are 3.89°C/s, 3.87°C/s, 3.83°C/s, and 3.79°C/s, respectively.
claims.quote: When the heating powers are 10 W, 15 W, 20 W and 25 W, the average cooling rates of the LIBs are 3.89°C/s, 3.87°C/s, 3.83°C/s, and 3.79°C/s, respectively. With the decrease of heating power, the inhibitory effect of LN on LIBs is weakened.

claims.claim: Vertical LN spraying (directed at the positive electrode) achieves a lower minimum battery temperature (−108.4°C) and a larger temperature reduction (330.6°C) compared to horizontal spraying, which achieves only −5.9°C minimum temperature and 259.7°C temperature reduction, when applied for 80–100 s at 20 W heating power and 100% SOC.
claims.quote: When the LN is sprayed vertically for 100 s, the lowest temperature of the battery reaches to −108.4°C. After spraying, the temperature returns to 38.9°C. By horizontal spraying at the time of 80 s, the lowest temperature of the battery is only −5.9°C, and the temperature of the battery rises to 114.1°C after spraying.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature (Tmax) during thermal runaway increases with SOC: batteries at 20%, 40%, 60%, 80%, and 100% SOC reach Tmax values of 307.7°C, 315.8°C, 344.6°C, 354.6°C, and 368.7°C, respectively, when heated at 20 W.
claims.quote: Tmax values of battery with 20%, 40%, 60%, 80% and 100% SOC are 307.7°C, 315.8°C, 344.6°C, 354.6°C, 368.7°C, respectively. Tmax increases with the increase of battery SOC.

claims.claim: The temperature reduction caused by LN cooling (ΔTm-r) increases with LN spraying time: at 20 s, 40 s, 60 s, 80 s, and 100 s, ΔTm-r values are 172.2°C, 246.3°C, 322°C, 331.9°C, and 432°C, respectively, for 100% SOC batteries.
claims.quote: the temperature reduction caused by LN cooling increases with the increase of LN spraying time... Time (s): 20, 40, 60, 80, 100 ... △Tm-r (°C): 172.2, 246.3, 322, 331.9, 432

claims.claim: The total heat accumulation of the overcharged large format LIB reaches 1971.0 kJ, with over 80% of it generated after thermal runaway (stage 3).
claims.quote: The total heat accumulation of the LIB reaches 1971.0 kJ, over 80% of which is generated after TR.

claims.claim: Under continuous overcharge current with water mist applied, thermal runaway occurrence is unstoppable, but the maximum surface temperature is reduced to 122.1 °C and over 1000 kJ of heat is dissipated during water mist release.
claims.quote: For cases with continuous overcharge current, the occurrence of TR is unstoppable, but the hazard has been significantly mitigated with the maximum temperature decreases to 122.1 °C, and over 1000 kJ heat has been dissipated during WM release.

claims.claim: The overcharge-induced LIB thermal runaway onset temperature is lower than that in thermally induced cases, because excess lithium deposits on the anode form lithium dendrites that cause internal short circuit and advance exothermic reactions.
claims.quote: the overcharge induced LIB TR presents lower self-heating temperature compared with the thermally induced cases... During the overcharge process, excess lithium deposits on the anode leading to the generation of lithium dendrites, which causes the internal short circuit and accelerates the hazard process.

claims.claim: The LIB thermal runaway is triggered at 124.8 °C with a maximum temperature rate of 5.3 °C/s under overcharge at 0.5 C, compared to the safety vent opening at 60 °C where exothermic side reactions cannot yet be activated.
claims.quote: The LIB TR is triggered at 124.8 °C... which increases to the maximum of 5.3 °C s−1. The safety vent opens at 60 °C, but the exothermic side reactions cannot be activated at such low temperature, indicating that the overcharge significantly accelerate the development of the hazard process.

claims.claim: The maximum water mist cooling factor reached only 7.8% in the test with thermal runaway occurrence during WM release, and only 3.4% when WM was applied after TR, indicating low cooling efficiency relative to the theoretical maximum cooling power of 267.9 kW.
claims.quote: the maximum fc reaches only 7.8% and 3.4%, respectively. Hence, even though the TR of overcharged LIB can be mitigated with WM, the efficiency should be further improved.

claims.claim: The maximum cooling capacity improvement with fine water mist addition reaches 29.5% compared with pure air flow cooling.
claims.quote: The maximum improvement reaches 29.5 %

claims.claim: When FWM flow rate exceeds 0.6 mL/s, local evaporation of droplets dominates the cooling process.
claims.quote: the local evaporation of droplets will dominate the cooling process when the FWM flow rate exceeds 0.6 mLs−1

claims.claim: With increasing air velocity, the retention time of droplets in the high temperature region is significantly shortened, leading to decreased cooling efficiency.
claims.quote: with the increase of air speed, the cooling capacity also increases, but the retention time of droplets in the high temperature region has been significantly shortened, leading to the decreased cooling efficiency

claims.claim: The LIB TR can be triggered under thermal, electrical, and mechanical abuse conditions, with the root cause lying in internal exothermic side reactions.
claims.quote: The LIB TR can be triggered under thermal, electrical, and mechanical abuse conditions, but the root cause lies in the internal exothermic side reactions

claims.claim: LIBs with 25% SOC were selected as test samples to avoid violent dynamic hazards such as ejection and explosion.
claims.quote: the LIBs with 25 % SOC were selected as the test samples in this experiment, where violent dynamic hazards (e.g., ejection and explosion) could be avoided

claims.claim: EIPCM with a core/matrix ratio of 2:1 (EIPCM3) exhibited a melting temperature of 51.01°C and a latent heat of 111.69 kJ/kg, which was the highest encapsulation efficiency among all tested ratios.
claims.quote: EIPCM with the core/matrix ratio of 2:1 exhibited the largest latent heat (111.69 kJ⋅kg−1) and suitable phase change temperature (51.01 ◦C) for battery thermal management applications.

claims.claim: At a high discharge rate of 3C, the peak battery module temperature decreased from 86.6°C (air cooling) to 66.1°C with EIPCM cooling, representing a 23.7% reduction.
claims.quote: the peak battery temperature from 86.6 ◦C to 66.1 ◦C at a high discharge rate of 3C

claims.claim: EIPCM delayed the onset of thermal runaway from 332 s to 827 s, a delay of 495 s, and reduced the peak battery surface temperature from 768.8°C to 574.7°C.
claims.quote: the trigger time of TR was further extended to 827 s... the peak battery surface temperature was reduced from 768.8 ◦C to 574.7 ◦C

claims.claim: Organic PCM (paraffin/EG) ignited after 5 seconds of flame contact and continued burning until the paraffin was exhausted, whereas EIPCM did not ignite even after 60 seconds of flame exposure.
claims.quote: the organic PCM was ignited easily after contacting the flame for merely 5 s, and the fire became fiercer until the paraffin ran out. By contrast, the EIPCM did not cause a fire even after igniting for 60 s.

claims.claim: Encapsulation of Na2HPO4·12H2O within silica reduced the mass loss rate during heating from 21.63%/min (pure hydrated salt) to 1.52%/min (EIPCM) after 100°C, indicating improved thermal stability.
claims.quote: showed a slower mass loss rate of 1.52%/min after 100 ◦C, which was much lower than that of Na2HPO4⋅12H2O of 21.63%/min

claims.claim: When EP/PR-MoS2-3 (3 mm thick EP/3.0 PR-MoS2 composite) is used as a barrier between 100% SOC NCM 811 pouch cells, thermal runaway propagation is effectively stopped and the temperature of the surviving battery remains below 100°C (T2max = 91.1°C).
claims.quote: When utilizing EP/PR-MoS2-3, no TR is found for battery 2, and T2max only reaches 91.1 ◦C. This is attributed to the superior flame retardant and thermal insulation properties of EP composite, effectively delaying and blocking the TRP of batteries.

claims.claim: When 1 mm EP (without flame retardant filler) is used as a barrier, TRP between 100% SOC LIBs is accelerated compared to the no-barrier case, with Δt1 reduced from 105 s (no barrier) to 52 s.
claims.quote: When 1 mm EP is used, the first battery experiences TR at 794 s, reaches T1max at 808 s. After heat transfer for 52 s, the battery 2 occurs TR at 846 s. Compared to the blank group (Δ t1 is 105 s), the usage of EP accelerates the TRP of two batteries. The thermal breakdown and combustion of EP increase the amount of heat surrounding the battery 2, thus accelerating up the progress.

claims.claim: With the addition of 3.0 wt% PR-MoS2 to epoxy resin, the peak heat release rate decreases by 56.7%, the peak CO production rate decreases by 56.6%, and the peak smoke production rate decreases by 24.7% compared to neat EP.
claims.quote: With the addition of 3.0 wt% PR-MoS2, the significant reductions of 56.7 %, 56.6 %, 24.7 % in PHRR, PCOP, PSPR, are observed, respectively.

claims.claim: Despite the surviving battery (battery 2) using EP-3 barrier and EP/PR-MoS2-3 barrier having only a 25°C difference in maximum temperature (T2max), the degree of internal side reactions differs significantly: battery 2 using EP-3 has Rct of 2.2 Ω and DLi+ of 2.920×10⁻⁸ cm²s⁻¹, while battery 2 using EP/PR-MoS2-3 has Rct of 0.0368 Ω and DLi+ of 1.057×10⁻⁴ cm²s⁻¹.
claims.quote: The degree of internal side reaction of batteries using EP-3 and EP/PR-MoS2-3 differ significantly, even though their T2max difference is only 25 ◦C.

claims.claim: In the no-barrier TRP test of 100% SOC NCM 811 pouch cells, after the first battery undergoes thermal runaway at 235.3°C with a maximum temperature of 701.6°C, the second battery undergoes thermal runaway 105 s later at 196.2°C reaching a maximum temperature of 792.6°C.
claims.quote: the TR of initial battery occurs at 235.3 ◦C and results in a maximum temperature of 701.6 ◦C. After 105 s, the second battery suffers TR at 196.2 ◦C. Of note, the maximum temperature of 792.6 ◦C is delivered.

claims.claim: EP/MAP-Cu20% coating applied to the surface of lithium-ion batteries reduces the surface temperature rise by 36% and delays the thermal runaway fire onset time by 51% compared to pure EP coating.
claims.quote: Compared to the pure EP, the EP/MAP-Cu prolongs by 51 % for t1, with Tmax and Rmax decreasing by 9.6 % and 36.4 %, respectively.

claims.claim: When EP/MAP-Cu20% coating of 3 mm thickness is applied to the external surface of the battery casing, the peak temperature of the external battery casing is only 93°C with a maximum rise rate of 2.8°C/s.
claims.quote: the peak temperature for EC3 is only 93 ◦C, with a maximum rise rate of merely 2.8 ◦C/s.

claims.claim: Increasing the EP/MAP-Cu20% coating thickness from 1 cm (IC1) to 3 cm (IC3) on the internal surface of the battery casing reduces the peak internal temperature by 31.6%, with IC1 reaching a peak of approximately 528°C.
claims.quote: The internal temperature for IC1 coating exhibits the fastest rise, reaching a peak of ~528 ◦C. In contrast, the temperature for the IC3 is effectively controlled, resulting in a peak temperature that is reduced by 31.6 % compared to IC1.

claims.claim: Increasing the proportion of MAP-Cu in the EP coating continuously reduces both THR and PHRR; at 20 wt% MAP-Cu, THR and PHRR are reduced by 74.4% and 76.7% respectively compared to pure EP.
claims.quote: the THR and PHRR values of EP/MAP-Cu20% are reduced by 74.4 % and 76.7 %, respectively, compared to pure EP.

claims.claim: When EP/MAP-Cu20% coating is applied to the internal side (IS) of the battery casing, the peak temperature is 319°C, which is lower than the 362°C peak temperature when the coating is applied to the top (IT) of the battery casing.
claims.quote: the peak temperature of IT reaches a higher value of 362 ◦C, while IS exhibits a more gradual temperature rise rate, with a peak temperature of only 319 ◦C.

claims.claim: When thermal runaway was triggered at cell1 (H-cell1) with no protective materials, all four cells completed thermal runaway within 15.94 s, averaging 5.31 s per cell.
claims.quote: The entire module completed thermal runaway in 15.94 s, averaging 5.31 s/cell.

claims.claim: H-cell3 (thermal runaway triggered at cell3) exhibited shorter propagation time (15.04 s), higher peak thermal runaway temperature (971.4 °C), and greater PHRR (89.85 kW) and THR (7.91 MJ) compared to H-cell1.
claims.quote: H-cell3 exhibited shorter propagation time (15.04 s), higher peak thermal runaway temperature (971.4 °C), and greater PHRR (89.85 kW) and THR (7.91 MJ).
