claims.claim: Using 1 mm SMEP alone (H-cell1-S-1) delayed thermal runaway propagation from cell1 to cell4 to a total interval of 133.42 s, but did not completely prevent propagation.
claims.quote: resulting a total cell1 to cell4 interval of 133.42 s. Using 1.5 mm SMEP (Fig. 10 a4-c4) further delayed propagation... Thus both 1 mm and 1.5 mm SMEP effectively delayed thermal runaway propagation compared to the unprotected case, but neither thickness completely prevented thermal runaway propagation.

claims.claim: A standalone 3 mm KGSA barrier (H-cell1-K-3) completely blocked thermal runaway propagation when triggered at cell1, with cell3 and cell4 surface temperatures peaking at only 75.8 °C and 46.5 °C respectively.
claims.quote: the thicker KGSA blocked most heat such that 2-F rose only to 313.2 °C by 606.30 s with no further increase and flame spread remained confined; heat transfer to cell3 and cell4 was much slower, with their surface temperatures peaking at only 75.8 °C (1921.60 s) and 46.5 °C (2788.04 s), respectively, indicating that the effective thermal barrier provided by the thicker KGSA prevented thermal runaway in cells 3 and 4.

claims.claim: The combination of 2 mm KGSA and 1.5 mm SMEP (H-cell2-KS-1.5 and H-cell3-KS-1.5) successfully prevented thermal runaway propagation under non-first-cell triggering conditions, achieving a minimum surface temperature of only 55.6 °C.
claims.quote: H-cell2-KS-1.5 and H-cell3-KS-1.5 not only reduced PHRR and THR values but also achieved a minimum surface temperature of only 55.6 °C, with neither cell experiencing thermal runaway propagation.

claims.claim: Although SMEP alone reduced PHRR, it increased total heat release (THR) by 50.00% (1 mm) and 110.55% (1.5 mm) compared to H-cell1, due to the PA content in SMEP enhancing combustion.
claims.quote: Whereas although S-1 and S-1.5 lower the PHRR their THR increases by 50.00 % and 110.55 % due to the PA content in SMEP that enhances combustion and raises THR.

claims.claim: Bidirectional thermal runaway propagation (H-cell3) significantly promoted heat generation, resulting in THR of 7.91 MJ versus 2.56 MJ for unidirectional propagation (H-cell1).
claims.quote: H-cell3 exhibited two similar HRR peaks (PHRR = 89.85 kW, THR = 7.91 MJ), attributable to the heating plate at the left of cell3 and resulting bidirectional propagation, indicating that the trigger location affects the heat distribution in the battery module, and bidirectional propagation significantly promotes heat generation during battery thermal runaway.

claims.claim: The combination of 2 mm KGSA and 1 mm SMEP (H-cell2-KS-1 and H-cell3-KS-1) delayed but did not fully suppress thermal runaway propagation under non-first-cell triggering, with propagation intervals of 752.10 s and 664.12 s respectively.
claims.quote: H-cell2-KS-1 and H-cell3-KS-1 effectively reduced PHRR and peak temperature, significantly delaying thermal runaway propagation time (752.10 s/664.12 s). However, due to excessive thermal shock intensity, they could not completely prevent thermal runaway spread, resulting in high heat release.

claims.claim: This study only investigated thermal runaway propagation in open environments, and the effectiveness of KGSA and SMEP in suppressing thermal runaway propagation in sealed environments requires further exploration.
claims.quote: However, this work only investigated the propagation characteristics of thermal runaway in battery packs under open environments. The effectiveness of KGSA and SMEP in suppressing thermal runaway propagation in sealed environments requires further exploration in future research.

claims.claim: With increasing IFR content in SMEP, the melting enthalpy gradually decreased from 115.78 J/g (SMEP1) to 102.67 J/g (SMEP4), while self-extinguishing combustion duration shortened from 150 s to 3 s.
claims.quote: the melting enthalpies of SMEP1–4 are 115.78 J/g, 111.14 J/g, 104.75 J/g, and 102.67 J/g, respectively... The combustion durations for SMEP1-SMEP4 were 150 s, 71 s, 60 s, and 3 s, respectively.

claims.claim: In the battery module without PCM, all cells underwent thermal runaway successively within 2 minutes under the influence of the trigger cell, with the peak temperature of cell 5 reaching 870.95 K and peak temperatures of other cells between 743 and 773 K.
claims.quote: Under the influence of the trigger cell, all the cells undergo TR successively within 2 min. The peak temperature of cell 5 reaches 870.95 K. The peak temperatures of the other cells are between 743 and 773 K.

claims.claim: In the battery module with PCM, the peak temperature of the trigger cell was significantly reduced to 635.54 K compared to 870.95 K without PCM, and thermal runaway chain reactions did not propagate to other cells.
claims.quote: Although cell 5 still undergoes TR, the peak temperature is significantly reduced to 635.54 K, and the temperature drops quickly. The TR process of the trigger cell is significantly suppressed... cell 5 does not seriously affect the temperatures of other cells, and the chain reactions of TR no longer occur in the battery module.

claims.claim: When PCM thermal conductivity increases from 0.28 to 12.0 W/(m·K), the maximum cell temperature during thermal runaway decreases from 704.26 K to 417.49 K, successively inhibiting the electrolyte decomposition reaction (above 473.15 K) and positive active material-solvent reaction (above 442.15 K).
claims.quote: when kp increases from 0.28 to 12.0 W/(m⋅K), Tmax decreases from 704.26 K to 417.49 K; thus, the electrolyte decomposition reaction and positive active material-solvent reaction are inhibited successively, and the heat generation is reduced.

claims.claim: When PCM thickness increases from 2 to 5 mm, T2min and QTR decrease significantly; however, when PCM thickness reaches 6 mm or greater, T2min and QTR no longer change significantly because the PCM is sufficient to completely absorb the heat released by the cell during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: when d increases from 2 to 5 mm, T2min decreases significantly. When d ≥ 6 mm, T2min hardly changes. This phenomenon occurs because the PCM is insufficient to completely absorb the heat released by the cell during TR when its thickness is between 2 and 5 mm... When d ≥ 6 mm, the heat released by the cell during TR can be completely absorbed by the PCM.

claims.claim: PCM thickness has little effect on the maximum cell temperature during thermal runaway because TR occurs in a very short time after penetration and the amount of PCM participating in heat absorption before the cell reaches maximum temperature is approximately unchanged regardless of thickness.
claims.quote: The thickness of the PCM has little effect on Tmax. The reason is that TR occurs in a very short time after the cell is penetrated. Subject to its low thermal conductivity, the amount of the PCM participating in the heat absorption before the cell reaches its maximum temperature is approximately unchanged, even if the thicknesses of the PCM are different.

claims.claim: Without effective suppression measures, all cells in the LIB array underwent thermal runaway within 5 seconds, with TR propagating from the first to the last cell in that interval.
claims.quote: The TR propagated rapidly in the LIB array without suppression measures, and all cells underwent TR within 5 s.

claims.claim: The TR propagation interval (optimum suppression stage) lasted only approximately 5 seconds (Δt = 5 ± 1 s).
claims.quote: The duration of the optimum suppression stage was very short, only about 5 s (Δt = 5 ± 1 s).

claims.claim: Intermittent spray with a period of 5 s (DC = 0.5) failed to prevent or delay thermal runaway propagation due to insufficient average cooling power during the suppression stage.
claims.quote: The cooling capacity of intermittent spray with a period of 5 s during TR interval was insufficient, which cannot prevent or delay thermal runaway propagation.

claims.claim: Continuous water spray with an effective spray time of 20 s could delay but not prevent TR propagation, with TR propagation average time of 197 s.
claims.quote: Although TR propagation in the LIB array was not prevented, continuous water spray exhibited an excellent cooling effect during the application, delaying TR propagation and eliminating the risk of TR propagation in the initial stage.

claims.claim: Intermittent spray with DC of 0.1 (Pt = 20 s) had the lowest water consumption and average cooling power during the optimal suppression stage and had no effect on suppressing TR propagation, with LIB array propagation time of 7 s.
claims.quote: intermittent spray with DC of 0.1 possessed the lowest water consumption and average cooling power during the optimal suppression stage, which had no effect on suppressing TR propagation. The LIB array propagation time was 7 s

claims.claim: Under passive FEPC release conditions, the thermal runaway onset temperature of LIB Cell1 was 130°C, representing a 35°C increase compared to the no-TM condition where Cell4 activated its vent at 95°C.
claims.quote: At the conclusion of Stage I, the LIB temperature reached 130°C, signifying that the TR temperature of LIB Cell 1, T2 = 130°C, marked the onset of TR and its subsequent propagation... Contrasting with scenarios lacking TM, FEPC elevated the TR temperature of LIBs to 130°C, an increase of 35°C.

claims.claim: Under proactive FEPC release conditions, the thermal runaway onset temperature of LIB Cell1 was 144°C, representing a 49°C increase compared to the no-TM condition.
claims.quote: The temperature at the conclusion of Stage I heating was T3 = 144°C, representing the uncontrolled temperature of LIB Cell1... under passive and proactive release conditions, the runaway temperature of LIBs increased by 35 and 49°C, respectively.

claims.claim: The passive TM approach delayed TR onset time by 30 s and increased TR completion time by 26 s, while the proactive TM approach delayed TR onset time by 80 s and increased TR completion time by 58 s.
claims.quote: the passive TM approach delayed the TR onset time by 30 s, increasing the TR completion time by 26 s. The proactive TM approach delayed the TR onset time by 80 s, increasing the TR completion time by 58 s.

claims.claim: The mass loss rate under no TM conditions was greater than under passive TM, which was greater than under proactive TM, with 180 g of FEPC reducing LIB mass loss ratio by 6% under passive release and 18% under proactive release.
claims.quote: φ no > φ passive > φ proactive... The use of only 180 g of FEPC could reduce the LIB mass loss ratio by 6 % and 18 %, respectively.

claims.claim: The microcapsule wall degradation onset temperature was 120°C, and the process largely concluded by 150°C with a weight loss rate exceeding 70%.
claims.quote: The onset of this process at 120°C signifies the initiation of microcapsule wall degradation, which largely concluded by 150°C, with a weight loss rate exceeding 70%.

claims.claim: The melting point of polymer shells used to encapsulate flame retardants is much higher than the onset temperature of abnormal heat generation in batteries, causing delayed flame retardant release and thermal inertia that further increases battery temperature.
claims.quote: the melting point of polymers is much higher than the onset temperature of abnormal heat generation in batteries. Thermal inertia after the polymer shell melts will further increase the battery temperature and cause the thermal shrinking of separators, thus resulting in serious short circuit

claims.claim: Stearic acid (PCM) has a melting point of 72°C, which is close to the onset temperature of abnormal heat generation in batteries, enabling prompt flame retardant release at the early stage of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Stearic acid, a PCM with a melting point (T m = 72 ºC) close to the onset temperature of abnormal heat generation in batteries, is used as the gating material for the controllable release of flame retardant.

claims.claim: When the TEP concentration in the electrolyte was 5% and 10%, the ionic conductivity of the PP separator decreased from 0.42 to 0.26 and 0.15 mS cm−1, respectively.
claims.quote: When the TEP concentration in the electrolyte was 5% and 10%, the ionic conductivity of the corresponding PP separator decreased from 0.42 to 0.26 and 0.15 mS cm−1, respectively

claims.claim: The PCM-TEP@SiO2/PP separator exhibits an ionic conductivity of 0.65 mS cm−1, which is higher than the PP separator with direct flame retardant addition at 5% or 10% TEP concentration.
claims.quote: the PCM-TEP@SiO2/PP separator exhibits a higher ionic conductivity of 0.65 mS cm−1

claims.claim: When the mass ratio of TEP/PCM was increased to 2:1, the TEP/PCM solution was not completely cured even after 5 min, indicating poor curing and packaging effect, whereas at ratios of 1:2 and 1:1 the solution promptly cured.
claims.quote: When the mass ratio of TEP/PCM was 1:2 and 1:1, the solution promptly cured. However, when the mass ratio of TEP/PCM was increased to 2:1, the TEP/PCM solution was not completely cured even after 5 min. Besides, it returned to a flowable liquid after shaking, indicating the poor curing and packaging effect of TEP in PCM.

claims.claim: CO2, CO, H2, and C2H4 are the primary gases generated at the initial TR triggering stage in NCM523/graphite prismatic lithium-ion batteries under adiabatic conditions.
claims.quote: CO2, CO, H2, and C2H4 have been proven to be the primary gases generated during the initial TR triggering stage

claims.claim: Most gas content remains stable after TR triggering in adiabatic thermal runaway tests of NCM523/graphite prismatic cells.
claims.quote: Most gas content remains stable after TR triggering

claims.claim: The ambient pressure value noticeably decreases during the initial heating stage (approximately 60–120°C) due to electrolyte vapor adsorption by porous electrode materials, and this pressure drop signal can be utilized for failure warning in real applications.
claims.quote: The ambient pressure value noticeably decreases due to the initial heating process, indicating that this signal can be utilized for failure warning in real applications. The primary mechanism is based on the electrolyte vapor adsorption effect: i) Temperature gradient and gas-phase adsorption: At the initial heating stage, the internal battery temperature increases in a gradient (generally 60–120°C), and organic solvents (such as EC and DMC) in the electrolyte begin to vaporize and diffuse into the electrode pores.

claims.claim: Oxygen consumption during severe combustion induces temporary fire suppression without jet fire, after which the Re-burn phenomenon occurs as generated and supplementary environmental oxygen flows along concentration gradients.
claims.quote: such fuel consumption causes temporary extinguishing, without jet fire. Moreover, the generated oxygen and supplementary oxygen from the environment flow along the oxygen concentration gradient directions, leading to the 'Re-burn' phenomenon.

claims.claim: ICP analysis determined that massive electrolytes immersed in electrode pieces provide fuel for the Remaining fire phenomenon after stable venting and jet fire, posing hazards to neighbouring untriggered cells.
claims.quote: ICP analysis reveals that massive electrolytes immersed in electrode pieces provide fuel for the 'Remaining fire' phenomenon after stable venting and jet fire, posing hazards to neighbouring untriggered cells.

claims.claim: Under 0.098 MPa sub-atmospheric gas extraction, TR triggering time is extended by 50 s and the maximum temperature of the second battery cell during TRP is decreased by 50.7°C compared to TRP without gas extraction, under the same environmental temperature and heating power.
claims.quote: the TR triggering time of each battery cell during the 0.098 MPa sub-atmospheric pressure based TRP suppression process is effectively prolonged (50 s) under the same environmental temperature and heating power triggering... The reduction in combustion intensity causes a decrease in Tmax of subsequent battery cells, Tmax of the second battery cell can be decreased by 50.7°C.

claims.claim: Under 0.098 MPa sub-atmospheric gas extraction at the single-cell level, the maximum temperature is decreased by 100°C and TR triggering time is extended by 40 s compared to no gas extraction, under the same ambient temperature (27°C) and lateral heating power (700 W).
claims.quote: with higher sub-atmospheric pressure, TR triggering time is significantly extended (40 s) under the same ambient temperature (27°C) and the same lateral heating power (700 W). Besides, the maximum temperature is significantly decreased by 100°C with more fuel-oxygen extraction

claims.claim: During calibration tests without fluorinated agents, the 18650 LIB reached a peak temperature of 623.2 °C and a peak pressure of 1.6 bar during thermal runaway at t = 257.1 min.
claims.quote: the temperature at t = 257.1 min is higly arised to 623.2 ℃ due to the thermal runaway. The decreasing of heating temperature could result in a slow decrease in the pressue history.

claims.claim: After addition of HT (hexafluoropropene trimer) cooling agent, the peak temperature increased to 821.3 °C and peak pressure increased to 6.1 bar, representing increases of 31.8%, 281.3%, and 601.9% in peak temperature, peak pressure, and peak pressure ratio respectively compared to the calibration test.
claims.quote: After addition of cooling agent into the chamber, the peak temperature and peak pressure caused by thermal runaway are highly increased to 821.3 ℃ and 6.1 bar, respectively. Compared with the calibration test, the peak temperature, peak pressure and peak pressure ratio are increased by 31.8 %, 281.3% and 601.9%, respectively.

claims.claim: Among the seven fluorinated agents tested, only HD and Novec 7500 decreased peak temperature (by 7.5% and 28.9% respectively), while HT, Novec 7100, Novec 7200, Novec 7300, and BTP all increased peak temperature during LIB thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The 7.5 % and 28.9 % decreasing of temperature are found when the HD and novec 7500 are incorporate into the chamber with a fixed concertration.

claims.claim: Addition of all seven fluorinated agents increased peak pressure during LIB thermal runaway, with HT causing the highest increase of 281.3% and BTP causing the lowest increase of 50.0%.
claims.quote: The peak pressure casued by the thermal runaway is increased by 281.3 %, 75.0 %, 56.2 %, 50.0 %, 112.5 %, 56.3% and 50.0 % when the HT, HD, 7100, 7200, 7300, 7500 and BTP are added, respectively.

claims.claim: The absence of hydrogen in the molecular structure of a fluorinated cooling agent is associated with a low temperature ratio, as demonstrated by the HT and HD cases, while high hydrogen content in the molecular structure leads to high flammability.
claims.quote: The absence of H in the molecular could contribute to the low temperature ratio, which agrees with cases of addition HT and HD to the chamber. It results in a low temperature ratio. Therefore, the H content of molecular impacts a big effect on the temperature ratio profiles. This is the same with the previous study that high content of H in the molecular could lead to a high flammability.

claims.claim: Forced air cooling systems provide heat transfer coefficients in the range of 10 to 50 W/m²·K, while optimized liquid cooled plates can reach values between 200 and 1000 W/m²·K.
claims.quote: Forced air systems typically provide heat transfer coefficients in the range of ten to fifty watts per square meter per kelvin, while optimized liquid cooled plates can reach values between two hundred and one thousand watts per square meter per kelvin.

claims.claim: Immersion cooling can improve heat dissipation efficiency by approximately 35% under controlled laboratory and pack level conditions, with corresponding reductions in peak cell temperature of up to 40% compared with air cooled and indirect liquid cooled systems.
claims.quote: Experimental investigations and simulation studies reported in the literature show that immersion cooling can improve heat dissipation efficiency by approximately thirty five percent under controlled laboratory and pack level conditions. Corresponding reductions in peak cell temperature of up to forty percent are observed when compared with air cooled and indirect liquid cooled systems.

claims.claim: Dispersing high thermal conductivity nanoparticles such as aluminum oxide, silicon carbide, and carbon nanotubes into dielectric base oils results in convective heat transfer enhancements on the order of 15 to 30%, accompanied by peak cell temperature reductions of approximately 25 to 30°C under controlled experimental conditions.
claims.quote: By dispersing high thermal conductivity nanoparticles such as aluminum oxide, silicon carbide, and carbon nanotubes into dielectric base oils, several studies have reported convective heat transfer enhancements on the order of fifteen to thirty percent. These improvements are accompanied by peak cell temperature reductions of approximately twenty five to thirty degrees Celsius under controlled experimental conditions and specific cell formats.

claims.claim: SEI breakdown begins at approximately 80–120°C, electrolyte decomposition occurs at approximately 130–200°C, and cathode oxygen release and gas ignition occur above 250°C during thermal runaway progression.
claims.quote: The critical temperature thresholds begin with SEI breakdown (~80 120 ◦C), followed by electrolyte decomposition (~130 200 ◦C), and culminate in cathode oxygen release and gas ignition (>250 ◦C).

claims.claim: During full scale thermal runaway, peak internal temperatures can exceed 800–1000°C, and internal pressure in sealed prismatic or pouch cells can exceed 600 kPa, often breaching casings and initiating mechanical ejection of molten materials.
claims.quote: Peak internal temperatures during full scale runaway can exceed 800 1000 ◦C, sufficient to ignite nearby modules and propagate failure across a battery pack. Simultaneously, gas evolution reactions produce hydrogen (H2), carbon monoxide (CO), methane (CH4), rapidly elevating internal pressure. In sealed prismatic or pouch cells, internal pressure can exceed 600 kPa, often breaching casings and initiating mechanical ejection of molten materials and vapor jets.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway propagation can reach speeds of 1 to 3 cells per second, as documented in infrared thermography studies, when thermal barriers are absent or ineffective.
claims.quote: leads to a chain reaction with propagation speeds reaching 1 3 cells/sec, as documented in infrared thermography studies.

claims.claim: HF emissions during thermal runaway can exceed 500 mg per Ah, a level necessitating the use of self-contained breathing apparatus for first responders, as documented by UL 9540A fire propagation testing.
claims.quote: UL 9540A fire propagation testing has documented HF emissions exceeding 500 mg per Ah a level necessitating the use of self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA) for first responders.

claims.claim: Immersion cooled packs can achieve temperature uniformity on the order of ±2–3°C under specific experimental conditions including defined cell format, flow rate, heat flux, and pack geometry.
claims.quote: ndicate immersion cooled packs can achieve temperature uniformity on the order of ± 2 3 ◦C under specific experimental conditions (cell format, flow rate, heat flux, and pack geometry), which interpreted in the context of boundary conditions and test protocols.

claims.claim: Thermal performance improvements from advanced cooling strategies, including immersion and nanofluid enhanced systems, cannot be directly generalized across all electric vehicle platforms or operating scenarios because they derive from studies conducted under controlled laboratory, module level, or pack level boundary conditions.
claims.quote: quantitative enhancements such as reductions in peak temperature or improvements in heat dissipation efficiency not directly generalized across all electric vehicle platforms or operating scenarios.

claims.claim: Even minimal temperature differentials as small as 5°C across the plane of large format pouch cells can lead to nonuniform aging, localized hot spot formation, and eventual thermal instability.
claims.quote: Advanced Multiphysics simulations and calorimetric diagnostics such as Accelerating Rate Calorimetry (ARC) and Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) have demonstrated even minimal temperature differentials, as small as 5 ◦C across the plane of large format pouch cells, can lead to nonuniform aging, localized hot spot formation, and eventual thermal instability.

claims.claim: Experiments with synthetic esters and mineral oils show improvements of up to approximately 30% in heat extraction over conventional liquid cooling.
claims.quote: Experiments with synthetic esters and mineral oils show improvements of up to ~ 30% in heat extraction over conventional liquid cooling.

claims.claim: Air cooling is unsuitable for high power or fast charging applications because its heat transfer coefficient of 10–50 W/m²·K causes thermal gradients that can exceed safety thresholds.
claims.quote: Air cooling, employing forced convection across finned surfaces or through channel flow, offers simplicity and low cost limited by its low heat transfer coefficient (10 50 W/m2⋅K). As a result, it is unsuitable for high power or fast charging applications thermal gradients can exceed safety thresholds.

claims.claim: Operation above 45°C accelerates battery degradation, while the optimal operating temperature range is 20–40°C; fast charging increases sensitivity to thermal stress.
claims.quote: Operation above 45 ◦C accelerates degradation optimal range is 20 40 ◦C. Fast charging increases sensitivity to thermal stress.

claims.claim: Nanofluid enhanced immersion cooling remains at the research and prototype stage due to unresolved challenges related to nanoparticle stability, dielectric degradation, filtration, and long-term reliability.
claims.quote: Despite promising laboratory scale results, nanofluid enhanced immersion cooling remains at and with unresolved challenges related to long-term stability, dielectric reliability, filtration, and manufacturability that currently limit near term mass deployment.

claims.claim: Separator shrinkage or melting at approximately 130–150°C leads to internal short circuits, amplifying localized Joule heating and accelerating the thermal runaway cascade.
claims.quote: Separator shrinkage or melting (~130 150 ◦C) lead to internal short circuits, amplifying localized Joule heating, and accelerating the cascade.

claims.claim: For 0% SOC cells undergoing thermal runaway, there were no jet flames observed.
claims.quote: They found that, for 0% SOC cells, there were no jet flames

claims.claim: As SOC rises, the battery's flame behavior during thermal runaway becomes more violent.
claims.quote: They concluded that, as the SOC rises, the more violent the battery's flame behavior is.

claims.claim: With increasing SOC, the mass loss ratio and highest temperature of lithium-ion cells are higher and the time when thermal runaway occurs decreases.
claims.quote: With the increase in the SOC, the mass loss ratio and highest temperature of lithium-ion cells are higher and the time when TR occurs decreased

claims.claim: There is not a monotonically increasing relationship between total heat release and SOC in lithium-ion batteries under external ignition.
claims.quote: However, there is not a monotonically increasing relationship between the total heat release and SOC.

claims.claim: The peak heat release rate (HRR) and the production of CO and HF increase with increasing SOC, indicating SOC plays an important role in causing thermal hazard.
claims.quote: It was discovered that the SOC of the cell has a significant influence on the peak HRR and the production of CO and HF increase with the increase in the SOC, indicating that the SOC plays an important role in causing thermal hazard.

claims.claim: Compared to 0% SOC and 50% SOC cells, the jet flow during thermal runaway was more violent in 100% SOC batteries, which aggravates thermal hazard.
claims.quote: it was found that, compared to 0% SOC and 50% SOC cells, the jet flow was more violent in 100% SOC battery, which aggravates thermal hazard.

claims.claim: The concentration of each gas generated during thermal runaway increases significantly with increasing SOC, especially CO and H2, posing a potential risk of fire and explosion.
claims.quote: Although there is little difference in the main components, the concentration of each gas increases significantly with the increase in the SOC, especially CO and H2, which poses a potential risk of fire and explosion in the battery system

claims.claim: Under gas production and flame conditions during thermal runaway, the maximum temperature of a 100% SOC battery was much higher than that of a 50% SOC battery.
claims.quote: It was found that, under the conditions of gas production and flame, the maximum temperature of the 100% SOC battery was much higher than that of the 50% SOC battery.

claims.claim: At the same temperature, the self-heating rate of aging batteries is higher, and lithium deposited on the anode during low-temperature aging greatly reduces the thermal stability of LIBs.
claims.quote: at the same temperature, the self-heating rate of aging batteries is higher, which shows that the lithium deposited on the anode during low-temperature aging will greatly reduce the thermal stability of LIBs.

claims.claim: A lower state of health (SOH) increased the risk of danger in LIBs because batteries with lower SOH values were more prone to fire accidents.
claims.quote: a lower SOH increased the risk of danger in LIBs to a certain extent, because batteries with lower SOH values were more prone to fire accidents.

claims.claim: The flame is almost not able to exist under 12 percent oxygen concentration for common hydrogen fuel gases.
claims.quote: the flame is almost not able to exist under 12 precent oxygen concentration for common hydrogen fuel gases

claims.claim: Lowering the oxygen concentration weakens the flame behavior when thermal runaway occurs in batteries.
claims.quote: it is obvious that lowering the oxygen concentration can weaken the flame behavior when TR occurs

claims.claim: Both argon and nitrogen dilution can slow down thermal runaway propagation by decreasing oxygen concentration, which reduces the flame heating and total heating values.
claims.quote: both argon and nitrogen dilution can slow down TRP, one essential reason for which being that decreasing the oxygen concentration through extra inert gas dilution can reduce the value of the flame heating (qf) and total heating (qt)

claims.claim: A completely confined space suppresses the maximum temperature of the battery due to insufficient combustion of the gases emitted from the battery.
claims.quote: it has been found that a completely confined space suppresses the maximum temperature of the battery due to the insufficient combustion of the gases emitted from the battery.

claims.claim: The total heat release (THR) value at high ambient pressure is relatively larger than at low pressure, and the unit growth rate of heat of combustion at both pressures increases with increasing SOC.
claims.quote: the ambient pressure has a significant effect on the heat of combustion, and the (total heat release) THR value at a high pressure is relatively larger than that at a low pressure. Also, the unit growth rate of the heat of combustion at both pressures increases with the increase in the SOC.

claims.claim: As ambient pressure decreases, the thermal runaway trigger time becomes longer and the maximum surface temperature decreases.
claims.quote: Wang et al. [61] found that, as the ambient pressure decreases, the TR trigger time becomes longer and the maximum surface temperature decreases.

claims.claim: The jet flame height during thermal runaway of 21700 LIBs reached a maximum at 50 kPa ambient pressure.
claims.quote: they found that the pressure had an impact on the height of the jet flame, which reached the maximum at 50 kPa.

claims.claim: The spray intensity of lithium-ion batteries at 30 kPa is significantly stronger than at 95 kPa due to the higher pressure difference at which the combustible material is released.
claims.quote: The spray intensity at 30 kPa is significantly stronger than that at 95 kPa due to the higher pressure difference at which the combustible material is released

claims.claim: High-rate overcharge of lithium iron phosphate batteries exhibited more severe ignition and ejection than low-rate (0.5 C) overcharge, evolving faster and appearing twice.
claims.quote: The results showed that the high-rate battery exhibited a more severe ignition and ejection than the low-rate one (0.5 C), not only evolving faster, but also appearing twice.

claims.claim: The battery exhibited greater thermal hazards at high charging rates and high ambient temperatures during charging.
claims.quote: the results showed that the battery exhibited greater thermal hazards at high charging rates and high ambient temperatures.

claims.claim: The flame behavior of thermal runaway triggered by mechanical abuse and electrical abuse is more intense than that triggered by thermal abuse, because the first two interact directly with the internal components of the battery.
claims.quote: the flame behavior of TR triggered by mechanical abuse and electrical abuse will be more intense than that by thermal abuse. This is because the first two interact directly with the internal components of the battery, which tends to result in a more violent reaction within the battery, leading to a greater release of energy.

claims.claim: In terms of potential hazard of thermal runaway, the ranking of battery cathode materials is LCO > NCM > LFP.
claims.quote: the results of the study show that LCO > NCM > LFP in terms of the potential hazard of TR

claims.claim: A parallel connection between cells increases the probability of thermal runaway propagation by exacerbating exothermic reactions within the cells, leading to higher heat flux to neighboring cells.
claims.quote: a parallel connection increases the probability of TRP. The main reason for this phenomenon is that the parallel connection exacerbates the exothermic reactions within the cells, leading to more violent combustion and energy release from the parallel cells. This results in a higher heat flux from the cell with TR to its neighboring cells, thus accelerating the TRP.

claims.claim: In a confined space during thermal runaway, the maximum heat flux from flame radiation was about four times that of open space due to the plate obstacle effect.
claims.quote: Comparing the heat flux in a confined space (CS) and open space (OS), it was found that the radiation heat from the flame was affected by the plate obstacle with larger values, and the maximum heat flux was about four times that of OS.

claims.claim: In a confined space, the fireball length before jet flame formation is larger for plate heights up to 10 cm and increases linearly with decreasing height.
claims.quote: the fireball length is larger for heights up to 10 cm and increases linearly with the decrease in height.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature of the environment in a confined space during thermal runaway propagation is much higher than in open space.
claims.quote: the confined space has a great influence on the ambient temperature, and the maximum temperature of environment in a confined space is much higher than that in open space

claims.claim: In a confined space, the TRP of the battery module is mainly contributed to by the ceiling flame, and the influence of the flame increased as the height decreased.
claims.quote: The TRP of the battery module mainly contributed to the ceiling flame in a confined space and the influence of the flame increased as the height decreased, indicating the energy from the flame was the most dominant heat source to trigger the TRP in a confined space.

claims.claim: Perfluorohexanone can quickly extinguish battery flames while CO2 takes longer, and perfluorohexanone achieves extinguishing through rapid cooling so the battery cannot reach the ignition point.
claims.quote: They found that perfluorohexanone could quickly extinguish battery flames, while CO2 took longer. Perfluorohexanone has a good extinguishing efficiency direct rapid cooling so that the battery cannot reach the point of ignition

claims.claim: Releasing water mist at the moment of safety valve cracking can achieve a fire extinguishing effect within a few seconds, whereas releasing water mist after battery thermal runaway results in an extinguishing time of about three minutes.
claims.quote: The results showed that releasing the WM at the moment of safety valve cracking could achieve a fire extinguishing effect within a few seconds, whereas by releasing WM after the battery TR, the extinguishing time was longer and took about three minutes.

claims.claim: Flood cooling is effective in suppressing both thermal runaway propagation and fire for LMO and LFP batteries, but less effective for NCM batteries.
claims.quote: They found that flood cooling is effective in suppressing both TRP and fire for LMO and LFP batteries, but less effective for NCM batteries.

claims.claim: A 4.5 m/s wind combined with 0.5 MPa water mist had the best suppression effect in a confined battery enclosure, achieving high heat dissipation capacity and reducing toxic and hazardous gas concentration.
claims.quote: The results showed that a 4.5 m/s wind and 0.5 MPa WM had the best suppression effect, which can achieve a high heat dissipation capacity and reduce the concentration of toxic and hazardous gases.

claims.claim: CO2 had better inhibitory effects on battery flame and explosion than N2 during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: They concluded that both CO2 and N2 had inhibitory effects on the battery flame and explosion, and CO2 had better effects than N2.

claims.claim: Adding flame-retardant Al(OH)3 to phase change material (PCM) delayed the ignition time and reduced the HRR as well as toxic gases.
claims.quote: The results showed that the flame-retardant additive played a positive role in delaying the ignition time and reducing the HRR as well as toxic gases

claims.claim: The combined use of flame-retardant phase change material (FRPCM) and aerogel felt effectively blocked thermal runaway propagation and suppressed the battery flame to a certain extent.
claims.quote: They found that the combined use of FRPCM and the aerogel felt effectively blocked TRP and suppressed the battery flame to a certain extent.

claims.claim: Early battery fire simulation models lacked venting and electrolyte vaporization processes, which prevented them from predicting exact thermal runaway behavior.
claims.quote: All models introduced above have a lack of venting and electrolyte vaporization, as a consequence of which they obviously cannot predict the exact TR behavior.

claims.claim: Neglecting radiative heat loss from solid particles and soot in CFD thermal runaway models possibly causes 20–40 percent overprediction in jet flame temperatures.
claims.quote: they indicated that the radiative heat loss from solid particles and soot was neglected in their model, as a consequence of which it possibly caused 20–40 percent overprediction in jet flame temperatures.

claims.claim: When there is no spacing between cells in an NCM battery module, heat transfer between neighboring cells is mainly through heat conduction and thermal radiation from the flame; when there is spacing, heat transfer is mainly through thermal radiation.
claims.quote: when there is no spacing between cells, the heat transfer between the neighboring cells is mainly through heat conduction and thermal radiation from the flame; when there is spacing between cells, the heat transfer between neighboring cells is mainly through thermal radiation

claims.claim: The change in SOC has a greater impact on thermal runaway flame ejection behavior in NCM battery modules than spacing between cells.
claims.quote: Zhu et al. [69] found that the change in the SOC has a greater impact on TR flame ejection behavior than spacing between cells.

claims.claim: In a confined space during thermal runaway, the total amount of combustible gases was greater than in open space, possessing larger potential deflagration risks and accelerating the speed of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the total amount of combustible gases was greater in a confined space than in open space. Therefore, it possesses larger potential deflagration risks, which will cause the speed of TR to be accelerated.

claims.claim: The rank of influence on thermal runaway risk from orthogonal tests was: charging rate > ambient temperature > aging.
claims.quote: the conclusion of the orthogonal tests indicated that the rank of the influence on the thermal runaway risk was charging rate > ambient temperature > aging.

claims.claim: The propagation speed of thermal runaway in air was quicker than under nitrogen conditions, indicating that jet fire accelerates the thermal runaway behavior of adjacent cells.
claims.quote: it was found that, compared to nitrogen condition, the propagation speed in air was quicker, which indicated that jet fire accelerates the TR behavior of adjacent cells.

claims.claim: LIBs perform best within a temperature range of 25–40 °C.
claims.quote: Studies have shown that LIBs perform best within a temperature range of 25–40 °C.

claims.claim: The temperature difference between cells in a battery module should be kept below 5 °C to ensure uniform performance and prevent rapid aging.
claims.quote: the temperature difference between cells in the battery module should be kept below 5 °C to ensure uniform performance and prevent rapid aging

claims.claim: Once a critical temperature threshold between 130 and 200 °C is surpassed, the battery can enter thermal runaway where temperature can escalate to 600–1000 °C, often accompanied by flammable gas emission, smoldering, and combustion.
claims.quote: Once a critical temperature threshold is surpassed, typically between 130 and 200 °C, the battery can enter TR, where the temperature can escalate to 600–1000 °C, often accompanied by the emission of flammable gases, smoldering, and combustion

claims.claim: Approximately 12% of the total heat released by the initiating battery is sufficient to trigger thermal runaway in adjacent cells, with the majority of heat transferring through the battery shell rather than the pole connector.
claims.quote: approximately 12% of the total heat released by the initiating battery is sufficient to trigger TR in adjacent cells, with the majority of heat transferring through the battery shell rather than the pole connector

claims.claim: Higher states of charge lead to faster and more intense thermal runaway propagation.
claims.quote: The intensity and speed of TRP are also influenced by the state of charge (SOC) of the batteries with higher SOCs, leading to faster and more intense TRP

claims.claim: When battery temperature surpasses 38°C, the microcontroller activates a relay to engage the fan to prevent hazardous operation.
claims.quote: When the temperature surpasses 38°C, a key threshold indicating potential overheating, the microcontroller will activate a relay to engage the fan, thereby preventing hazardous operation.

claims.claim: When battery temperature exceeds 38°C during testing, the microcontroller disables both relays to terminate the power supply, with a response time below 0.5 seconds, significantly mitigating the risks of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: To avert additional heating, the microprocessor swiftly evaluates the signal and disables both relays to terminate the power supply, as depicted in Fig. 7. The response time, recorded at below 0.5 seconds, significantly mitigated the risks of thermal runaway.

claims.claim: In slow charging mode, the system provides 5V and maintains a charging current of approximately 0.52 A, achieving a full charge in 4 to 6 hours depending on battery capacity.
claims.quote: Upon activation of a relay, the system provides 5V in slow charging mode, maintaining a consistent charging current of around 0.52 A. This method exhibited thermal efficiency, producing negligible heat and attaining a full charge in 4 to 6 hours, depending on battery capacity.

claims.claim: In rapid charging mode, engaging both relays facilitates a current flow of approximately 1.3 A, decreasing the charging time to two to three hours, though this mode generates a considerable amount of heat.
claims.quote: Engaging both relays facilitated a current flow of roughly 1.3 A in rapid charging mode, hence decreasing the charging time to two to three hours. The system functioned within safe thermal limits, even though this mode generated a considerable amount of heat.

claims.claim: Overcharge protection severs the power supply when the voltage surpasses 4.2V per cell, preventing overcharging and ensuring optimal battery performance.
claims.quote: To ensure the battery remains within safe parameters, overcharge protection is incorporated, severing the power supply when the voltage surpasses 4.2V per cell.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway in LIBs can begin at temperatures as low as 60°C, typically initiated between the anode and electrolyte.
claims.quote: thermal runaway can begin at relatively low temperatures and is usually initiated between the anode and electrolyte [33,49], This breakdown can begin at temperatures as low as 60 ◦C (140 ◦F).

claims.claim: At approximately 100°C, the electrolyte begins to break down and react with intercalated lithium atoms, generating flammable hydrocarbon gases.
claims.quote: The electrolyte will begin to break down and react with the intercalated lithium atoms at around 100 ◦C.

claims.claim: At approximately 130°C, the polymer separator begins to melt, leading to cathode and anode shorting and further temperature increase.
claims.quote: Once the temperature reaches around 130 ◦C, the polymer separator begins to melt, leading to the cathode and anode shorting out and further increasing the temperature of the cell.

claims.claim: Without external cooling, thermal runaway fire occurred every time during thermal abuse of pouch-type LIBs charged to approximately 90% SOC.
claims.quote: Results showed that, without cooling, a thermal runaway fire occurred every time during the thermal abuse.

claims.claim: External water spray cooling applied at 100°C successfully prevented thermal runaway in pouch-type LIBs subjected to thermal abuse.
claims.quote: external cooling successfully prevented thermal runaway. This observation shows that using water as a temperature reducer is more effective than using it as a fire suppressant.

claims.claim: When battery temperature exceeds 100°C due to a short circuit, PTC in a high resistance state protects individual batteries from the influence of overcurrent.
claims.quote: when the battery's temperature exceeds 100 ◦C due to a short circuit, PTC in a high resistance state protects individual batteries from the influence of overcurrent.

claims.claim: The P3BT-523 composite electrode hardly discharged after exposure to 150°C for 5 minutes, indicating that PTC could suppress internal short circuits.
claims.quote: The P3BT-523 composite electrode hardly discharged after exposure to 150 ◦C for 5 min, thus indicating that PTC could suppress internal short circuits.

claims.claim: A failure of PTC in a battery pack may result in ignition and thermal runaway, as confirmed by evidence that a PTC in a 14-cell series package was ignited.
claims.quote: A failure of PTC may result in ignition and TR, as confirmed by evidence that a PTC in a 14-cell series package was ignited.

claims.claim: Both K2 18650E and LG M36 CID types could be activated at 100°C, with activation pressures of 1.781 ± 0.355 MPa and 1.799 ± 0.284 MPa, respectively.
claims.quote: both types of CID could be activated at 100 ◦C, with activation pressures of 1.781 ± 0.355 and 1.799 ± 0.284 MPa, respectively.

claims.claim: High-voltage (>50 V) CID could generate arc under overcharge conditions, and sparks and heat generated by the arc will ignite combustible gas and the battery electrolyte, resulting in thermal runaway.
claims.quote: high-voltage (>50 V) CID could generate arc under overcharge conditions, and contact surface might be erosion or ablation by high current density. Sparks and heat generated by the arc will ignite combustible gas and the battery electrolyte, which result in TR in the battery

claims.claim: A single large (>100 Ahr) lithium-ion cell at 3.5 Vdc, when short circuited, can reach temperatures exceeding 200 °F (93 °C) within a few seconds.
claims.quote: A single large (>100 Ahr) lithium-ion cell at 3.5 Vdc, short circuited, can reach temperatures exceeding 200 °F (93 °C) within a few seconds.

claims.claim: The potential for ignition during a thermal runaway event is reduced at 30% SOC compared with a higher-SOC cell, but an off-gassing lithium-ion cell produces a large quantity of toxic gases even at 30% SOC.
claims.quote: The potential for ignition during a thermal runaway event is reduced at 30% SOC compared with a higher-SOC cell. An off-gassing lithium-ion cell produces a large quantity of toxic gases even at 30% SOC.

claims.claim: Short-circuit events in lithium-ion cells can produce 20–30 C discharge current instantaneously for a very short duration (less than 1 second) and 8–10 C sustained discharge current.
claims.quote: testing data for various lithium-ion cell manufacturers have shown that short-circuit events can produce 20–30 C discharge current instantaneously for a very short duration (<1 second) and 8–10 C sustained discharge current.

claims.claim: Most industry-accepted DC arc flash incident energy calculation methods for lithium-ion battery packs yield conservative results that are magnitudes higher than actual values seen in physical tests.
claims.quote: most industry-accepted methods yield conservative results, which in many cases are magnitudes higher than actual values seen in physical tests.

claims.claim: Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Specification 305 requires a minimum of 500 ohms/volt AC and 100 ohms/volt DC isolation between the chassis ground and high-voltage conductors, which equates to a minimum isolation of 200 kilohms on a 400-Vdc system.
claims.quote: Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Specification 305 requires a minimum of 500 ohms/volt ac and 100 ohms/volt dc isolation between the chassis ground and high-voltage conductors for customer and service worker safety on vehicles. This equates to a minimum isolation of 200 kilohms on a 400-Vdc system.

claims.claim: The thermosensitive microcapsule (TM) fire extinguishing agent reduces the peak thermal runaway temperature of a single-cell LIB by 14.0 °C and decreases the heating rate by 0.17 °C/s compared to no extinguishing agent.
claims.quote: the peak TR temperature of a single-cell LIB is reduced by 14.0 ◦C, and the heating rate is decreased by 0.17 ◦C/s

claims.claim: When the LIB temperature reaches 120 °C, the MUF wall material of the TM ruptures and releases the encapsulated core fire extinguishing materials.
claims.quote: When the temperature of LIBs exceeds 120 ◦C, the core material of the TM fire extinguishing agent coated on the surface of the LIBs is automatically released, effectively reducing the temperature and mitigating TR risks.

claims.claim: The TM displays a broad endothermic peak ranging from 51 to 119.0 °C with a maximum peak at 90.8 °C, and upon exceeding 119 °C the structural integrity of the wall material is compromised, resulting in release of the core material.
claims.quote: The TM displays a broad endothermic peak ranging from 51 to 119.0 ◦C, with a maximum peak at 90.8 ◦C...Upon exceeding a temperature of 119 ◦C, the structural integrity of the wall material is compromised, resulting in the subsequent release of the core material.

claims.claim: In the dual-LIB module test with TM applied, cell body 1 reached a maximum temperature 18.0 °C lower than the highest temperature under natural cooling, and cell body 2 reached a peak of only 63 °C, which is 6.0 °C lower than without suppression.
claims.quote: This temperature is 18.0 ◦C lower than the highest temperature of natural cooling...Compared with the natural cooling to suppress the TR of the LIBs, the peak value is reduced by 6.0 ◦C

claims.claim: The Br element in 2-BTP captures free radicals within the combustion chain reaction, thereby disrupting the oxidation process and reducing the formation of flammable gases, but 2-BTP remains stable below 120 °C preventing premature decomposition during normal operation.
claims.quote: Free radicals within the combustion chain reaction can be effectively captured by the Br element in 2-BTP, thereby disrupting the oxidation process and ultimately reducing the formation of flammable gases...2-BTP remains stable below 120 ◦C, preventing premature decomposition during normal operation.

claims.claim: Intermittent injection of 6.66 kg LN resulted in a maximum post-injection battery temperature of 69.7 ± 6.4°C and absorbed 441.2 ± 7.8 kJ of heat from the TR battery, compared to continuous injection of the same dose which resulted in 81.6 ± 7.9°C and absorbed 428.5 ± 9.7 kJ.
claims.quote: The maximum average post-injection temperature of the battery is 81.6 ± 7.9°C under continuous injection and 69.7 ± 6.4°C under intermittent injection, highlighting the superior inhibition effect of intermittent LN injection.

claims.claim: When LN injection dose was below 5 kg, it did not effectively inhibit thermal runaway, resulting in insufficient control over battery temperature.
claims.quote: we observed that when the LN injection is below 5 kg, it doesn't effectively inhibit TR, resulting in insufficient control over the battery temperature.

claims.claim: As LN injection dose increased from 5.72 kg to 8.02 kg under intermittent injection, the cooling rate of the TR battery increased from 0.609 ± 0.012 to 0.955 ± 0.019 and heat absorbed increased from 326.5 ± 6.5 kJ to approximately 1.62 times that value.
claims.quote: The cooling rate of the TR battery increased from 0.609 ± 0.012 to 0.955 ± 0.019 with intermittent injections ranging from 5.72 kg to 8.02 kg of LN.

claims.claim: At the anomalous warming stage (95°C battery temperature), injection of only 1.76 kg of LN prevented thermal runaway with a maximum post-injection temperature of 68.6°C, whereas at TR initiation (135°C), 6.66 kg of LN was required to achieve a maximum post-injection temperature of 72.5°C.
claims.quote: when the anomalous warming stage (95°C), 1.76 kg of LN is injected, resulting in a maximum battery post-injection temperature of 68.6°C. As the battery begins to TR (135°C), 6.66 kg of LN is injected, yielding a maximum post-injection temperature of 72.5°C.

claims.claim: When the same amount of LN is injected at higher battery temperatures (135°C vs 320°C at TR onset), the total heat absorbed by LN remains almost unchanged, but the maximum post-injection battery temperature rises and the cooling rate decreases.
claims.quote: when the same amount of LN is injected, the total heat absorbed by the LN remains almost unchanged as the battery temperature increases. However, the maximum post-injection temperature of the battery rises, and the cooling rate of the battery decreases.

claims.claim: The SEI layer on the anode surface first decomposes at a temperature roughly above 90 °C, leading to exposure of the lithiated anode which then reacts with organic solvents of the liquid electrolyte, generating flammable hydrocarbon gases such as methane and ethane.
claims.quote: the SEI layer on the anode surface first decomposes at a temperature roughly above 90 °C. The decomposition of SEI leads to the exposure of the lithiated anode (or lithium metal). The exposed anode then reacts with the organic solvents of the liquid electrolyte, generating flammable hydrocarbon gases such as methane, ethane, and others

claims.claim: When cell temperature is above approximately 130 °C for PE or 160 °C for PP, the polyethylene or polypropylene separator begins to melt and shrink, causing an internal short circuit and further deterioration of the battery.
claims.quote: When the cell temperature is above ≈130 °C/160 °C, the polyethylene (PE)/polypropylene (PP) separator begins to melt and shrink, which causes an internal short circuit and further deterioration of the battery.

claims.claim: The lithium-transitional metal oxide cathode decomposes at a temperature of approximately 180 °C, releasing oxygen.
claims.quote: the lithium-transitional metal oxide cathode decomposes at a temperature of ≈180 °C, releasing oxygen.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway stage 3, the maximum battery temperature can reach as high as 1000 °C.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature can reach as high as 1000 °C.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway in one battery can easily propagate to adjacent batteries in battery packs or modules due to their close vicinity and a relatively adiabatic environment, causing devastating failure of the entire system.
claims.quote: TR in one battery can easily propagate to the adjacent batteries in battery packs or modules, due to their close vicinity and a relatively adiabatic environment, causing the devastating failure of the entire system.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway can raise lithium-ion battery cell temperature to over 800 °C in a few seconds under mechanical stress, electrical overcharging, or external thermal conditions.
claims.quote: LIBs are prone to thermal runaway (TR) under mechanical stress, electrical overcharging, or external thermal conditions, resulting in a fast and self-propagating reaction that can raise the cell temperature to over 800 °C in few seconds

claims.claim: In confined spaces, thermal runaway consequences are significantly more severe than in open environments due to restricted heat dissipation and enhanced gas-phase reactions that exacerbate flame propagation and pressure accumulation.
claims.quote: Prior research indicates that the repercussions of TR are significantly more severe in confined areas than in open environments, attributable to restricted heat dissipation and enhanced gas-phase reactions that exacerbate flame propagation and pressure accumulation

claims.claim: Ultrafine water mist (UWM) is defined by droplet diameters of approximately 10 µm.
claims.quote: ultrafine water mist (UWM) – defined by droplet diameters of approximately 10 µm

claims.claim: Due to their comparatively low momentum, ultrafine droplets exhibit restricted penetration and distribution in high-temperature regions.
claims.quote: due to their comparatively low momentum, ultrafine droplets exhibit restricted penetration and distribution in high-temperature regions

claims.claim: The volume-based mean diameter (D32) of the UWM was 12.314 µm, while that of the conventional fine water mist was 119.043 µm.
